questions.question: Which line removes duplicate rows from a pandas DataFrame df?
options: df = df.drop_duplicates()
df = df.unique()
df = df.remove_duplicates()
df = df.dropna()
questions.correctAnswer: df = df.drop_duplicates()
questions.explanation: drop_duplicates() is the built-in pandas method for de-duplicating rows.
questions.hint: Use the pandas method specifically made for dedupe.

questions.question: Which method converts values to datetime in pandas?
options: to_datetime()
as_date()
parse_date()
datetimeify()
questions.correctAnswer: to_datetime()
questions.explanation: pd.to_datetime() is the standard conversion utility in pandas.

questions.question: What does df['col'].isnull().sum() return?
options: Count of non-null values in col
Count of null values in col
Percentage of null values in col
Boolean mask of null values
questions.correctAnswer: Count of null values in col
questions.explanation: isnull() creates a boolean mask where nulls are true, and sum() counts true values.

questions.question: What is the most important difference between a script you run manually and a pipeline that runs unattended at 03:00?
options: The pipeline must handle errors gracefully, log what it does, and make failures diagnosable from logs alone
The pipeline must run faster than any human-operated script to fit within the maintenance window allotted
The pipeline must use less memory than scripts so it can run on the smallest available cloud instance
The pipeline must be written in a single file so the on-call engineer can read it without an IDE
questions.correctAnswer: The pipeline must handle errors gracefully, log what it does, and make failures diagnosable from logs alone
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: pipelines run when nobody is watching. Error handling, logging, and testability are the disciplines this course teaches beyond basic Python.

questions.question: Why is Python the default starting language for data engineering teams?
options: Widest access to tools, jobs, and teams — most frameworks (Airflow, dbt, PySpark) use it as primary interface
Fastest execution speed — Python compiled bytecode outperforms Scala on data engineering workloads
Smallest memory footprint — Python interpreters use less RAM than the JVM by a factor of ten
Strictest type system — Python's type checker catches every runtime error before deployment to production
questions.correctAnswer: Widest access to tools, jobs, and teams — most frameworks (Airflow, dbt, PySpark) use it as primary interface
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: Scala/Java/Go fit specific cases. Python is the default — and it gives you the widest ecosystem (Airflow, dbt, PySpark all use it).

questions.question: What does this course assume you can already do in Python?
options: Write a for loop, define a function, use a dictionary, and read a file with open()
Build a Flask web application, deploy it to AWS Lambda, and serve traffic at production scale
Write Cython extensions to optimize hot loops below the speed of standard library implementations
Implement a custom asyncio event loop and a coroutine scheduler from scratch in pure Python
questions.correctAnswer: Write a for loop, define a function, use a dictionary, and read a file with open()
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: this course builds on basic Python foundations. If for/function/dict/open feel unfamiliar, start with a general Python course first.

questions.question: Why is the CelticWind scenario a deliberate simplification of a real energy company?
options: The goal is to learn Python patterns, not the energy industry — every simplification is intentional
Lithuanian energy regulations require all training data to omit certain fields per the curriculum
Apache Python community style guides forbid using realistic data sets in any educational material
The Python interpreter cannot handle the row counts of a real energy company without crashing
questions.correctAnswer: The goal is to learn Python patterns, not the energy industry — every simplification is intentional
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: scenarios expand only where complexity is needed (timezone, API pagination). Real companies have thousands of fields; CelticWind keeps the focus on Python patterns.

questions.question: Each CelticWind data source has a 'personality' that teaches a different Python skill. Why?
options: CSV teaches text parsing, Parquet teaches schemas, nested JSON teaches navigation, the API teaches paginated auth
Each source uses a different programming language internally and the Python wrapper is unique to each one
Each source is owned by a different department at CelticWind so the data formats reflect their preferences
Each source charges a different licensing fee so the format mirrors the cost structure of the contract terms
questions.correctAnswer: CSV teaches text parsing, Parquet teaches schemas, nested JSON teaches navigation, the API teaches paginated auth
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: the scenario includes all five so each Python skill has a natural use case. Different sources teach different skills.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind use the Bronze/Silver/Gold layered architecture instead of writing CSV → billing summary directly?
options: The three-layer approach forces validation before aggregation — every Gold record passed quality gates first
The Apache Iceberg specification requires three named layers for any data warehouse to be considered compliant
Bronze and Silver are required by Lithuanian energy regulators as auditable intermediate stages of every pipeline
The three-layer pattern is enforced by the file system because S3 buckets cannot have fewer than three folders
questions.correctAnswer: The three-layer approach forces validation before aggregation — every Gold record passed quality gates first
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: 'works until it doesn't' — a bad value (daily_kw='N/A') slips through to billing without validation. Layers prevent the most expensive mistake: wrong results that look right.

questions.question: Why is the pipeline function in Lesson 7 only 12 lines?
options: Each called function (read, validate, transform, write) hides its complexity behind a clear name
The pipeline is a stub — the real implementation has 200 lines and lives in a private Git repository
The Apache Airflow scheduler imposes a 12-line maximum on top-level pipeline orchestration code
The pipeline avoids comments and whitespace to stay below CelticWind's strict line-count budget at all costs
questions.correctAnswer: Each called function (read, validate, transform, write) hides its complexity behind a clear name
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: modular design = pipeline reads top to bottom without knowing how each function works internally. Module 4 teaches you to write code this way.

questions.question: How do the Python libraries in this course connect to other data engineering frameworks?
options: csv/json skills lead to PySpark readers; logging leads to Airflow logging; pytest leads to dbt testing
The course's libraries are CelticWind-specific and have no connection to any external framework or tool
Each library is a thin Python wrapper around an Apache Spark Java class loaded through Py4J at runtime
Frameworks use these libraries directly so you can replace any framework call with the underlying Python
questions.correctAnswer: csv/json skills lead to PySpark readers; logging leads to Airflow logging; pytest leads to dbt testing
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: each library opens a door to a framework skill. Learning these libraries builds the foundation every framework assumes you have.

questions.question: How does the Python for Data Engineers track relate to the SQL for Data Engineers track?
options: Independent — neither requires the other; taking both gives you the complete foundation if you use both
Sequential — Python must be completed before SQL because every SQL example uses Python helper functions
Identical — both tracks teach the same content with different code samples for each scenario
Replacement — the SQL track is being deprecated and will be merged into Python during the next quarter
questions.correctAnswer: Independent — neither requires the other; taking both gives you the complete foundation if you use both
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: independent tracks (CelticWind for Python, HydroAlpes for SQL). If your team uses both, take both. If only Python, this track alone is enough.

questions.question: What does Module 2 begin teaching after Module 1's foundation?
options: Python toolbox — virtual environments, project structure, type hints, configuration, and pre-commit hooks
PySpark fundamentals — DataFrame operations, aggregations, joins, and the Spark execution engine
Airflow scheduler internals — how the metadata database stores DAG runs and task instance state
Cloud architecture — AWS Lambda functions, S3 buckets, and the IAM permissions data pipelines need
questions.correctAnswer: Python toolbox — virtual environments, project structure, type hints, configuration, and pre-commit hooks
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: Module 2 covers the toolbox — venv, dependencies, project layout, modules, type hints, YAML config, env vars, __main__, linting, project template.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind require `python3 -m venv .venv` as the first command in every project setup?
options: Installing without a venv modifies system Python — pip upgrades can break OS package managers and IDEs
Virtual environments are required by Lithuanian regulations for any production data pipeline globally
Without a venv, Python refuses to import any third-party packages from PyPI as a security default
venvs run 3× faster than the system Python because they bundle pre-compiled bytecode for all imports
questions.correctAnswer: Installing without a venv modifies system Python — pip upgrades can break OS package managers and IDEs
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: virtual envs isolate the project's packages from the system Python. First command in every CelticWind setup guide.

questions.question: What does `pip freeze > requirements.txt` produce?
options: A pinned list of every package + exact version installed in the active environment
An optimized binary wheel for each package the project uses, ready to deploy to production
A dependency graph showing which packages depend on which other packages transitively
A security audit report of every package's CVE history aggregated from public vulnerability databases
questions.correctAnswer: A pinned list of every package + exact version installed in the active environment
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: requirements.txt is the project's shopping list. Anyone clones + pip install -r = identical tool set to the original developer.

questions.question: What is the value of CelticWind's standard project layout (src/, tests/, config/, data/)?
options: A new engineer can find things without asking — validators in validators.py, tests in tests/, config in config/
The Apache Python style guide mandates this exact directory structure for every package on PyPI
Python's import system requires this layout to resolve modules correctly across platforms reliably
The pyproject.toml file refuses to install packages whose source layout differs from this template
questions.correctAnswer: A new engineer can find things without asking — validators in validators.py, tests in tests/, config in config/
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: a good layout answers questions before they are asked. New engineers don't need to ask 'where is X?' — the structure predicts.

questions.question: What is the rule that prevents circular import errors at CelticWind?
options: Modules import downward (pipeline imports readers; readers imports nothing from pipeline)
Modules import alphabetically (a-modules before b-modules) so the resolution order is predictable
Modules import lazily (every import statement is wrapped in try/except ImportError as a fallback)
Modules import dynamically (importlib.import_module() at runtime instead of static import statements)
questions.correctAnswer: Modules import downward (pipeline imports readers; readers imports nothing from pipeline)
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: if readers imports validators and validators imports readers, Python raises ImportError. Downward-only imports break the cycle.

questions.question: What does the type hint `def filter_readings(readings: list[dict], min_kw: float, region: str) -> list[dict]` give you that a comment does not?
options: Verifiable contract — mypy and the IDE compare hints against actual usage and warn on mismatches
Faster execution speed — Python skips runtime type checking when hints are present in the signature
Encrypted parameters — the type checker encodes argument names so they are not visible in stack traces
Auto-generated tests — pytest produces input examples for every type hint and runs them automatically
questions.correctAnswer: Verifiable contract — mypy and the IDE compare hints against actual usage and warn on mismatches
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: a comment is unverifiable. mypy + IDE check hints against usage. CelticWind caught 3 bugs in the first month from refactors that changed return types but not all callers.

questions.question: What is the rule about credentials in `config.yml`?
options: Never — config.yml is committed to Git and appears in the repo's history forever
Sometimes — only the production config.yml may store credentials but staging/dev versions cannot
Always — every credential should be checked into Git so the team can audit who changed what when
Optionally — credentials may be stored encrypted with a team-shared key inside the YAML file itself
questions.correctAnswer: Never — config.yml is committed to Git and appears in the repo's history forever
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: API keys, DB passwords, auth tokens never appear in config files. Lesson 7 covers env vars + .env for secrets.

questions.question: What must appear in `.gitignore` before any commit?
options: .env — committing it once exposes every secret in Git history forever, even if deleted later
.venv — virtual environments are reproducible from requirements.txt and waste storage in the repo
src/ — the source directory is rebuilt from a private artifact registry on each deployment automatically
tests/ — test files contain sensitive validation logic that should not be visible in the public repo
questions.correctAnswer: .env — committing it once exposes every secret in Git history forever, even if deleted later
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: a single .env commit exposes secrets in Git history forever. CelticWind's pre-commit hook rejects any commit that includes a file named .env.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `if __name__ == '__main__':` at the bottom of a Python file?
options: Code runs only when the file is executed directly, not when it is imported as a module
Code runs only on the main thread, not in any subprocess started by the multiprocessing module
Code runs only on the master node of a distributed cluster, not on the worker nodes during execution
Code runs only when the Python interpreter is the default system interpreter, not a virtual environment
questions.correctAnswer: Code runs only when the file is executed directly, not when it is imported as a module
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: above the line = library definitions. Below = actual execution. File becomes both reusable library (import) and executable program (run).

questions.question: Why does CelticWind use a pre-commit hook for ruff and black instead of asking developers to run them?
options: Automation is more reliable than good intentions — especially at 03:00 hotfixing a pipeline
Pre-commit hooks run faster than manual invocation because they cache results between commits
ruff and black require root permissions to modify files, which only the pre-commit hook has
Manual invocation produces formatting that differs slightly from the automated tools' output
questions.correctAnswer: Automation is more reliable than good intentions — especially at 03:00 hotfixing a pipeline
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: developers forget. Some skip when in a hurry. Pre-commit removes the choice — runs automatically, fails commit if code does not pass.

questions.question: What does CelticWind's project template provide for new pipelines?
options: Scaffolding — venv, directory structure, .gitignore, dependencies, and pyproject.toml ready to fill
Production code — a fully implemented pipeline that the new engineer customizes for their use case
Documentation only — markdown files explaining how to set up a project from scratch by hand
Cloud infrastructure — Terraform modules that deploy AWS resources before any code is written
questions.correctAnswer: Scaffolding — venv, directory structure, .gitignore, dependencies, and pyproject.toml ready to fill
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: the foundation phase output. Module 3 starts filling the scaffolding with pipeline logic — data structures for tabular data.

questions.question: What is the safest way to access a field that might not exist in a dictionary?
options: reading.get('substation', 'UNKNOWN') — returns the default if the key is absent without raising
reading['substation'] or 'UNKNOWN' — returns the default if the value is None or empty string
reading.fetch('substation', default='UNKNOWN') — Python's preferred way for safe field access
try reading['substation'] except: 'UNKNOWN' — wraps the access in a try/except for safety
questions.correctAnswer: reading.get('substation', 'UNKNOWN') — returns the default if the key is absent without raising
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: dict.get(key, default) returns the default when the key is missing. Bracket access raises KeyError on missing keys.

questions.question: What is the memory cost of representing 400,000 rows × 5 fields as a list of dictionaries?
options: 2 million copies of the key strings — each dict stores its keys alongside its values
Roughly 400 bytes total — Python's dict implementation deduplicates keys across instances
Identical to a list of named tuples — Python optimizes the layout for both data structures
Lower than CSV input — Python compresses dict values when stored in a list larger than 1000
questions.correctAnswer: 2 million copies of the key strings — each dict stores its keys alongside its values
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: each dict copies its keys. For under 1M rows it is negligible; for larger sets, named tuples and dataclasses (lessons 6-7) are more efficient.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind keep filter functions non-mutating (every filter creates a new list)?
options: Each step's input stays unchanged so each step is independently debuggable
Python's garbage collector cannot release memory if a list is mutated in place during iteration
List comprehensions are required by the Python standard to be non-destructive in all cases
Mutation is forbidden by the type checker because list[dict] is declared immutable in the type system
questions.correctAnswer: Each step's input stays unchanged so each step is independently debuggable
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: a bug in step 3 may have been caused by a mutating step 1. Keeping each step's input unchanged makes every step independently debuggable.

questions.question: What property does the `transform_reading` function in Lesson 4 demonstrate?
options: Purity — output depends only on input; no files, databases, globals, or other external state
Concurrency — the function runs in parallel across multiple CPU cores by default in Python 3.12
Compilation — the function is JIT-compiled to machine code on the first call and cached afterwards
Encryption — the function encrypts the output dictionary using a key derived from the input
questions.correctAnswer: Purity — output depends only on input; no files, databases, globals, or other external state
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: pure functions are easy to test (known input → known output) and easy to debug (behavior fully determined by arguments). Module 4 explores this in depth.

questions.question: Why is `defaultdict(float)` useful for region-level totals?
options: Auto-initializes any new key to 0.0 — no need to check 'if region not in totals' before adding
Stores values in a sorted order by default so the output is naturally alphabetized by region
Encrypts the values at rest so the totals cannot be read without the right decryption key
Persists to disk automatically every time a value is updated for the duration of the program
questions.correctAnswer: Auto-initializes any new key to 0.0 — no need to check 'if region not in totals' before adding
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: defaultdict(float) returns 0.0 for missing keys on first access. Eliminates the 'if key not in dict: dict[key] = 0' boilerplate.

questions.question: What advantage do named tuples have over dictionaries for 400,000 meter readings?
options: Memory — field names stored once in the class, not 400,000 times — roughly 40% memory savings
Speed — field access by name is 10× faster than dict key lookup at the Python interpreter level
Validation — named tuples enforce field types automatically without any decorator declarations
Encryption — named tuple fields are encrypted in memory and decrypted only on attribute access
questions.correctAnswer: Memory — field names stored once in the class, not 400,000 times — roughly 40% memory savings
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: dict stores keys per instance; named tuple stores them once in the class. ~40% memory savings on CelticWind's 400K daily readings.

questions.question: What does `__post_init__` enable in a dataclass?
options: Validation and normalization after fields are set — runs once per instance at construction time
Persistence to disk — the instance is automatically serialized to a file after initialization completes
Pre-computation of derived fields — Python caches the values at class definition time, not per instance
Memoization of method calls — every method's return value is cached based on the input arguments
questions.correctAnswer: Validation and normalization after fields are set — runs once per instance at construction time
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: __post_init__ runs after __init__ finishes setting fields. Perfect place for validation (raise) and normalization (self.region = self.region.upper()).

questions.question: When should you choose a dataclass over a dictionary for a meter reading?
options: Silver layer — schema is known, validation is wanted, immutability or normalization is desired
Bronze layer — raw data with unknown schema needs the flexibility of dynamic field names
Network transfer — sending records over the network requires dataclass serialization headers
Configuration — runtime settings are mutable and rarely need the type contract of a dataclass
questions.correctAnswer: Silver layer — schema is known, validation is wanted, immutability or normalization is desired
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: dict for unknown schemas (Bronze). Dataclass for known schemas with validation (Silver). Trust the dataclass = downstream code does not re-check.

questions.question: What is the most common bug with `itertools.groupby`?
options: Forgetting to sort the input first — unsorted data produces multiple groups for the same key
Forgetting to convert each group to a list — the lazy iterator can be consumed only once safely
Forgetting to import groupby from itertools — Python's standard library has another similar function
Forgetting to filter None values first — groupby raises TypeError on None keys silently otherwise
questions.correctAnswer: Forgetting to sort the input first — unsorted data produces multiple groups for the same key
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: groupby groups consecutive rows with the same key. DUBLIN, CORK, DUBLIN unsorted = 3 groups. Always sorted_data = sorted(data, key=key_func) first.

questions.question: What does Module 3's pipeline pattern (`validate_and_transform` → `aggregate_by_region`) demonstrate?
options: Each function takes input, produces output, has a single clear responsibility — Module 4's preview
Each function runs in a separate Python process for true parallelism on multicore machines
Each function caches its result in Redis so repeated calls return instantly without recomputation
Each function is automatically tested by pytest's discovery mechanism without any test fixtures
questions.correctAnswer: Each function takes input, produces output, has a single clear responsibility — Module 4's preview
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: single-responsibility functions are the foundation of the pipeline pattern. Module 4 makes this the foundation of every function you write.

questions.question: Why is a function with one responsibility easier to test?
options: Give it a known input, check the output — no files, validation rules, or output paths needed
It runs faster — single-purpose functions are auto-compiled by Python's interpreter at startup
It uses less memory — Python releases the function's stack frame immediately after the return
It is shorter — fewer lines means fewer test cases needed to cover all the function's branches
questions.correctAnswer: Give it a known input, check the output — no files, validation rules, or output paths needed
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: a function that reads, validates, transforms, and writes needs files, rulesets, configs, and writable paths to test anything. Single-responsibility = testable.

questions.question: What does the `*` in `def filter_readings(readings, *, min_kw=0, max_kw=5000):` enforce?
options: Everything after `*` is keyword-only — callers must name min_kw and max_kw explicitly
Everything after `*` is variadic — the function accepts any number of positional arguments
Everything after `*` is required — no defaults are allowed for parameters past the asterisk
Everything after `*` is type-checked — mypy enforces stricter type rules on these parameters
questions.correctAnswer: Everything after `*` is keyword-only — callers must name min_kw and max_kw explicitly
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: keyword-only enforces calls like filter(readings, min_kw=0). filter(readings, 0, 5000) raises TypeError. Forces readable call sites.

questions.question: What does `tuple[list[dict], list[dict]]` document about a function's return value?
options: Returns two lists — typically (valid, rejected) — that the caller unpacks with `valid, rejected = ...`
Returns a tuple containing one list of dicts — the second `list[dict]` is the type alias name
Returns either a tuple or two lists depending on whether the input was non-empty when the call ran
Returns a list whose elements are tuples — the outer `tuple` is metadata about the iteration order
questions.correctAnswer: Returns two lists — typically (valid, rejected) — that the caller unpacks with `valid, rejected = ...`
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: tuple[X, Y] = function returns 2 values. Caller does `valid, rejected = validate_readings(...)`. The hint communicates the contract.

questions.question: Where in the pipeline architecture do impure functions belong?
options: At the boundaries — readers (input) and writers (output) — with pure functions in the middle
Throughout the pipeline — every function should perform some I/O for observability and logging
At the top level — only `if __name__ == '__main__':` may invoke functions with side effects
Inside try blocks — impure functions raise too many exceptions to live outside error handlers
questions.correctAnswer: At the boundaries — readers (input) and writers (output) — with pure functions in the middle
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: impure at edges (read/write), pure in the middle (validate/transform/aggregate). Pure middle = majority of code is testable without mocking files or APIs.

questions.question: When should you use a generator expression `(...)` instead of a list comprehension `[...]`?
options: When you only iterate once and want to avoid storing the entire intermediate list in memory
When you need to index the result by position, like `result[0]` or `result[-1]` afterwards
When the function will be called from multiple threads concurrently and needs thread-safety
When you want pytest to discover the values automatically and run a test for each one
questions.correctAnswer: When you only iterate once and want to avoid storing the entire intermediate list in memory
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: list comprehension builds the full list in memory. Generator expression yields one at a time — laziness saves memory for single-pass operations like sum(...).

questions.question: What is the key behavior of a generator (a function that uses `yield`)?
options: Produces values one at a time without storing the whole sequence — file is never fully loaded
Produces values in parallel across multiple CPU cores using Python's multiprocessing module
Produces values cached in Redis so repeated iteration returns the cached values instantly
Produces values type-checked at runtime by mypy before each value is yielded to the caller
questions.correctAnswer: Produces values one at a time without storing the whole sequence — file is never fully loaded
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: yield produces values lazily. Each row is read, transformed, yielded, then the next row is processed. File is never fully in memory.

questions.question: When does CelticWind's style guide prefer `lambda` over a list comprehension?
options: With sorted/min/max where the `key=` argument is the established Python pattern
With map and filter — lambdas are required because comprehensions cannot wrap them safely
With pytest fixtures — fixtures must be lambdas because they cannot be regular functions
With dataclass __post_init__ methods — lambdas are the only way to validate dataclass fields
questions.correctAnswer: With sorted/min/max where the `key=` argument is the established Python pattern
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: comprehensions for filter and transform; lambda only with sorted/min/max where key= is the standard pattern. [r['region'] for r in readings] reads better than map+lambda.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind organize code into readers.py, validators.py, transformers.py, and writers.py?
options: Each source module has a corresponding test module — one-to-one mapping shows where tests live
Each module is automatically deployed to a separate AWS Lambda function for parallel execution
Each module has a different Python interpreter version optimized for its specific workload type
Each module is owned by a different team at CelticWind so the boundaries reflect organizational scope
questions.correctAnswer: Each source module has a corresponding test module — one-to-one mapping shows where tests live
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: validators.py has test_validators.py. Module boundary = test boundary. New function in validators.py = new test in test_validators.py.

questions.question: What should a docstring document according to CelticWind's style guide?
options: The business reason behind the code — `max_kw=5000 reflects 5 kW nameplate × 1000` — not what the code does
The exact line-by-line behavior — every branch, every variable, every return statement, fully described
The performance characteristics — Big-O complexity, memory usage, and worst-case execution time per call
questions.correctAnswer: The business reason behind the code — `max_kw=5000 reflects 5 kW nameplate × 1000` — not what the code does
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: code already says what; docstring says why. The next engineer can read Python; they cannot read the business requirement that motivated it.

questions.question: What does Module 4's `run_silver_pipeline` function demonstrate as a pattern?
options: Composition — high-level orchestration calls focused single-responsibility functions from each module
Inheritance — every function inherits from BasePipeline and overrides one method per layer
Concurrency — every step runs in a separate thread with synchronization barriers between layers
Memoization — every function caches its results so re-running the pipeline returns instantly
questions.correctAnswer: Composition — high-level orchestration calls focused single-responsibility functions from each module
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: pipeline orchestration calls read → validate → transform → write. Each function does one thing. The pattern is composition.

questions.question: What is the most important thing to remember about CSV files?
options: Every value is a string — '28.5' is the string '28.5', not the number 28.5
Every value is a float — Python's csv module auto-converts numeric values to float on read
Every value is encoded — CSV files require base64 decoding before any field can be accessed
Every value is unique — duplicate rows are automatically de-duplicated when DictReader runs
questions.correctAnswer: Every value is a string — '28.5' is the string '28.5', not the number 28.5
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: every CSV value is a string. reading['daily_kw'] > 0 compares strings (alphabetical), not numbers — silent wrong results without error.

questions.question: What does `csv.DictWriter` do that `csv.writer` does not?
options: Writes from dictionaries with field names as columns — no positional mapping needed
Writes encrypted output by default using the column names as the encryption key derivation
Writes only the rows that have changed since the last write to the same output file path
Writes JSON output with the fields converted to JSON object keys instead of CSV columns
questions.correctAnswer: Writes from dictionaries with field names as columns — no positional mapping needed
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: DictReader + DictWriter = consistent dict representation throughout the pipeline. Rename a field once; both readers and writers see the new name.

questions.question: An Austrian legacy CSV uses ISO-8859-1 encoding and semicolons. What is the right approach?
options: Pass `encoding='iso-8859-1'` and `delimiter=';'` to the reader — both as configurable parameters
Convert the file to UTF-8 and replace all semicolons with commas before reading it with Python
Open the file in binary mode and decode each line manually using the chardet library auto-detection
Reject the file — Python's csv module supports only UTF-8 with comma delimiters by default
questions.correctAnswer: Pass `encoding='iso-8859-1'` and `delimiter=';'` to the reader — both as configurable parameters
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: assumptions of UTF-8 and comma are wrong for European data. CelticWind's readers accept encoding and delimiter as parameters with defaults.

questions.question: What does `json.load` give you that `csv.DictReader` does not?
options: Type preservation — 28.5 is the float 28.5, not the string '28.5', because JSON has number type
Schema enforcement — JSON files validate every value against a registered schema by default
Compression — JSON files are smaller than CSV because Python's json module compresses on read
Pagination — json.load streams records one at a time so the entire file never enters memory
questions.correctAnswer: Type preservation — 28.5 is the float 28.5, not the string '28.5', because JSON has number type
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: JSON has number, bool, null types. CSV has only strings. JSON eliminates string-to-number conversion errors that plague CSV pipelines.

questions.question: Why does `json.dumps({'timestamp': datetime.now()})` raise TypeError?
options: datetime is not JSON-serializable — use `default=str` or a custom serializer to handle it
datetime requires a timezone — naive datetimes cannot be JSON-encoded under any circumstances
json.dumps requires a list at the top level — wrap the dict in `[...]` first to fix the error
json.dumps cannot encode dictionaries with non-string keys — convert keys to str before encoding
questions.correctAnswer: datetime is not JSON-serializable — use `default=str` or a custom serializer to handle it
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: datetime is not in JSON's type set. default=str converts it to a string; custom serializer gives precise control over the format.

questions.question: How does CelticWind handle nested JSON in the pipeline?
options: Flatten immediately at the Bronze-to-Silver boundary — all subsequent processing uses flat dicts
Preserve the nesting throughout — every transformation function navigates the nested structure
Convert to XML — XPath expressions are easier than dictionary navigation for deeply nested data
Store raw JSON in the database — let the database handle the navigation through JSON path queries
questions.correctAnswer: Flatten immediately at the Bronze-to-Silver boundary — all subsequent processing uses flat dicts
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: complexity of navigation is isolated in the flattening function. Pipeline core works with flat dicts regardless of source format.

questions.question: Why is Parquet preferred over CSV for Silver and Gold layers at CelticWind?
options: Type preservation, columnar storage, compression — every analytical platform uses it as native format
Apache Spark refuses to read CSV files in production environments larger than 1 GB on disk
Lithuanian regulations require all data above the Bronze layer to use the Apache Parquet format
Parquet is the only format that supports ACID transactions out of the box without extra tooling
questions.correctAnswer: Type preservation, columnar storage, compression — every analytical platform uses it as native format
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: CSV is Bronze (raw); Parquet is Silver/Gold (validated, typed, compressed). Spark, Delta, Iceberg all use Parquet as native storage.

questions.question: Why should you specify a Parquet schema explicitly instead of letting pyarrow infer it?
options: Inferred types may vary between runs — int64 today, float64 tomorrow when a decimal arrives
Inferred types are slower because pyarrow scans the entire file to determine each column type
Inferred types raise warnings that pollute the Airflow logs and obscure the real pipeline output
Inferred types are encrypted by default and require a key the pipeline does not normally have
questions.correctAnswer: Inferred types may vary between runs — int64 today, float64 tomorrow when a decimal arrives
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: schema drift between runs breaks downstream consumers. Explicit schema = consistent types across all writes. Define once in config, reuse for every write.

questions.question: What advantage does `pathlib.Path` provide over string paths?
options: Cross-platform path joining with `/` operator — works on Windows, macOS, and Linux identically
Auto-validation that the file exists — raises FileNotFoundError when constructing the Path
Auto-encryption — Path objects encrypt the file's contents transparently on read and write
Multi-threading — Path objects support concurrent reads from multiple threads without locking
questions.correctAnswer: Cross-platform path joining with `/` operator — works on Windows, macOS, and Linux identically
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: Path('/data') / 'bronze' / file works on all platforms. open(), pyarrow, most libs accept Path directly. CelticWind keeps Path objects throughout, strings only at config boundary.

questions.question: What does Lesson 10's `read_data_file` function demonstrate?
options: Format detection from suffix — same function for CSV, JSON, Parquet returning list[dict] uniformly
Format conversion — every input file is converted to Parquet first, then read into Python dicts
Format compression — every input file is gzipped before reading to reduce memory footprint
Format validation — every input file is checked against a JSON schema before any data is loaded
questions.correctAnswer: Format detection from suffix — same function for CSV, JSON, Parquet returning list[dict] uniformly
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: dispatch on suffix. .csv → DictReader, .json → json.load, .parquet → pyarrow. Pipeline gets list[dict] regardless of source format.

questions.question: Why is `datetime.now()` (without `timezone.utc`) dangerous in pipeline code?
options: Naive datetimes depend on the machine's local timezone — different timestamps on dev vs prod
Naive datetimes are immutable and cannot be passed to any standard library function correctly
Naive datetimes raise DeprecationWarning in Python 3.12+ and will be removed in Python 3.14
Naive datetimes round to seconds — microsecond precision is lost in the conversion to UTC
questions.correctAnswer: Naive datetimes depend on the machine's local timezone — different timestamps on dev vs prod
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: laptop in Dublin (winter UTC+0) vs Frankfurt server (winter UTC+1) produces different timestamps from the same code. Always datetime.now(timezone.utc).

questions.question: What does the string `'01/02/2025'` mean in a CSV file?
options: Ambiguous — January 2 in US format (MM/DD), February 1 in European format (DD/MM)
Always January 2 — the United States date format is the global standard for ISO 8601 dates
Always February 1 — European date format is the only valid CSV format per RFC 4180 specification
Always invalid — proper CSV files use ISO 8601 (YYYY-MM-DD), so the slash format is rejected
questions.correctAnswer: Ambiguous — January 2 in US format (MM/DD), February 1 in European format (DD/MM)
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: same string, two interpretations. Wrong format = every date in Jan-Dec is wrong silently. CelticWind documents the format per source in YAML.

questions.question: What is the recommended format for storing a date in a database or Parquet file?
options: ISO 8601 via `isoformat()` — universal standard, lossless roundtrip via `fromisoformat()`
Unix timestamp as string — Python's preferred format for cross-platform date persistence
Custom format like `15-Jan-2025` — human-readable everywhere and easy to debug at 03:00
Excel serial number — interoperable with the spreadsheet tools the analytics team uses daily
questions.correctAnswer: ISO 8601 via `isoformat()` — universal standard, lossless roundtrip via `fromisoformat()`
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: isoformat() for machines, strftime() for humans/file systems. ISO 8601 is universal and roundtrips losslessly via fromisoformat().

questions.question: What does `timedelta` handle and not handle in date arithmetic?
options: Handles days, hours, minutes, seconds — not months or years (because 'one month' is ambiguous)
Handles months and years — not weeks (use `timedelta(weeks=1)` syntax which is unsupported)
Handles only days — Python's standard library cannot do sub-day arithmetic with timedelta
Handles all date units — including business days and holidays through the standard library
questions.correctAnswer: Handles days, hours, minutes, seconds — not months or years (because 'one month' is ambiguous)
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: 'one month from January 31' is ambiguous (Feb 28? March 3?). Use dateutil.relativedelta for months. timedelta covers most pipeline needs.

questions.question: Why is comparing `'2025-01-15T23:00:00'` (Irish) and `'2025-01-16T00:00:00'` (CET) as strings wrong?
options: These strings represent the same moment in time but compare unequal as strings
These strings have different lengths so Python's comparison operator fails to convert them
These strings use different separators that Python's string comparator handles incorrectly
These strings contain different timezone abbreviations that mask the true UTC offset value
questions.correctAnswer: These strings represent the same moment in time but compare unequal as strings
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: 23:00 UTC+0 = 00:00 UTC+1 = same UTC moment. Naive string comparison says 'different' (wrong) and 'earlier' (wrong). UTC normalization fixes both.

questions.question: What is the difference between `replace(tzinfo=...)` and `astimezone(...)`?
options: `replace` attaches a timezone label without changing the time; `astimezone` converts the time value
`replace` requires a string timezone name; `astimezone` requires a `timezone` object reference
`replace` is read-only; `astimezone` mutates the original datetime in place when called
`replace` is for dates; `astimezone` is for datetimes — they are mutually exclusive operations
questions.correctAnswer: `replace` attaches a timezone label without changing the time; `astimezone` converts the time value
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: replace = label naive datetime with its source TZ. astimezone = convert to a different TZ. Confusing them silently shifts every timestamp.

questions.question: What are the three steps of CelticWind's timezone normalization function?
options: Parse to naive (fromisoformat) → label with source TZ (replace) → convert to UTC (astimezone)
Parse to UTC (fromisoformat) → label with target TZ (replace) → convert to naive (astimezone)
Read from DB → cache in Redis → write to UTC table — the normalization is database-side only
Strip the timezone (replace) → convert to local time (astimezone) → format as ISO string (isoformat)
questions.correctAnswer: Parse to naive (fromisoformat) → label with source TZ (replace) → convert to UTC (astimezone)
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: parse → label → convert. Skip step 2 = naive treated as system local TZ (wrong). Skip step 3 = stays in source TZ (comparisons fail).

questions.question: What is the UTC-first pattern's rule?
options: Convert every timestamp to UTC at the Bronze-to-Silver boundary; all downstream code uses UTC
Convert every timestamp to local time at read time; consumers convert back to UTC if needed
Convert every timestamp to a Unix epoch integer; UTC is implicit in the epoch representation
Convert every timestamp to the source's timezone; compare only timestamps from the same source
questions.correctAnswer: Convert every timestamp to UTC at the Bronze-to-Silver boundary; all downstream code uses UTC
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: normalize at Bronze→Silver. All Silver/Gold code works in UTC. Joins, comparisons, aggregations work correctly across multi-timezone sources.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind use `date` objects (not strings) for date arithmetic in partitioning?
options: Date objects handle calendar quirks correctly — strftime only at the I/O boundary for Path
Date objects use 50% less memory than strings — important for partitions with many distinct dates
Date objects support arithmetic operators that Python strings have removed in recent versions
Date objects are required by pyarrow's schema — string dates are rejected during Parquet writes
questions.correctAnswer: Date objects handle calendar quirks correctly — strftime only at the I/O boundary for Path
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: date math (Feb 28 + 1 day = March 1) is correct on date objects, broken on strings. Format only when constructing the Path.

questions.question: Why does the meter-to-price matching function in Lesson 10 work?
options: Both reading and price are normalized to UTC during Bronze→Silver, so timestamp equality works
Both reading and price are stored in the same database table that auto-aligns timezones on read
Both reading and price use the same source timezone so the comparison is timezone-independent
Both reading and price are floats representing the Unix epoch with no timezone metadata at all
questions.correctAnswer: Both reading and price are normalized to UTC during Bronze→Silver, so timestamp equality works
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: matching depends on consistent UTC representation. Without normalization, 23:00 Irish ≠ 00:00 CET in string form, and the join fails.

questions.question: What does Module 7 close from Module 1's six gaps between script and pipeline Python?
options: Error handling, logging, and graceful failure — making pipelines diagnosable from logs alone
Type hints, docstrings, and code style — making pipeline code consistent across the team
Configuration, secrets, and environment management — separating code from environment
Testing, mocking, and CI integration — proving the pipeline works through automated tests
questions.correctAnswer: Error handling, logging, and graceful failure — making pipelines diagnosable from logs alone
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: Module 1 listed 6 gaps. Module 7 closes the error/logging/recovery gap. Every function: handles expected errors, logs behavior, fails gracefully.

questions.question: Why is `except:` (or `except Exception:`) a bad pattern in pipeline code?
options: Catches programming bugs (NameError, TypeError) that should crash the pipeline, not be hidden
Catches network errors twice — once at the OS level and once at the Python application level
Catches keyboard interrupts and prevents the engineer from stopping a runaway pipeline gracefully
Catches deprecation warnings that the Python type checker needs to surface to the developer
questions.correctAnswer: Catches programming bugs (NameError, TypeError) that should crash the pipeline, not be hidden
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: bare except hides code defects (NameError, TypeError) silently. Catch specific types (FileNotFoundError, ValueError). Let bugs crash so they get fixed.

questions.question: How should you decide which exceptions to catch?
options: Data errors (FileNotFound, ValueError, ConnectionError) — catch. Code errors (NameError, TypeError) — let crash.
All exceptions — every function should catch every exception and log it for debugging purposes
No exceptions — let every error crash and let the orchestration layer (Airflow) handle retries
Only exceptions documented in the function's docstring — undocumented errors should not be caught
questions.correctAnswer: Data errors (FileNotFound, ValueError, ConnectionError) — catch. Code errors (NameError, TypeError) — let crash.
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: data errors (external world is imperfect) → handle. Code errors (bugs) → let crash so they get fixed. Catching TypeError hides bugs instead of fixing them.

questions.question: What does CelticWind's `CelticWindError` base class enable?
options: Single `except CelticWindError` clause catches all pipeline-specific errors but lets bugs crash
Automatic retry on every subclass — Python retries the failing operation 3 times by default
Encrypted error messages — the base class encrypts the exception's message field for security
Performance optimization — the base class caches stack traces so subsequent raises are faster
questions.correctAnswer: Single `except CelticWindError` clause catches all pipeline-specific errors but lets bugs crash
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: base class lets `except CelticWindError` catch all pipeline errors. TypeError (Python built-in) escapes — caller knows it is a code bug, not data.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind use `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` instead of print statements?
options: Logger output has level (INFO/ERROR), timestamp, module name, and goes to file + console configurably
Logger output is faster than print — print blocks on stdout while logger writes asynchronously
Logger output is encrypted by default while print sends plaintext to the terminal application
Logger output is required by Lithuanian regulations for any production data pipeline globally
questions.correctAnswer: Logger output has level (INFO/ERROR), timestamp, module name, and goes to file + console configurably
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: logger writes structured output to file/console with level, timestamp, module name. print produces unstructured output that monitoring cannot parse.

questions.question: Where should logging be configured at CelticWind?
options: Once, in the pipeline's `if __name__ == '__main__':` block — before any module creates a logger
In every module file — each module configures its own logger with module-specific format strings
In the YAML config — Python's logging module reads logging.yml directly without any code involved
In the conftest.py — pytest configures logging globally for both test runs and production runs
questions.correctAnswer: Once, in the pipeline's `if __name__ == '__main__':` block — before any module creates a logger
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: configure once at entry point. Modules call getLogger(__name__) and inherit. Configuring inside modules may not propagate to other modules' loggers.

questions.question: What should every log message answer?
options: What happened, to what data (which meter, which file), and what was the result
Who wrote the code, when they committed it, and which engineer is currently on call
Which Python version is running, which OS the pipeline lives on, and which CPU architecture
Which Airflow DAG ID, which task ID, and which execution date triggered the function
questions.correctAnswer: What happened, to what data (which meter, which file), and what was the result
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: log reader has no context. Every message: what (operation), to what data (meter/file), result (count/duration/error). Code-required-to-understand = bad message.

questions.question: Which HTTP status codes warrant retries?
options: Transient: 503 (unavailable), 429 (rate limit), 502/504 (gateway), Timeout, ConnectionError
Permanent: 401 (auth), 400 (bad request), 404 (not found) — the response will not change
All 5xx codes — server-side errors are always retriable per HTTP RFC 7231 specification
All 4xx codes — client-side errors are retriable when the client adjusts the request format
questions.correctAnswer: Transient: 503 (unavailable), 429 (rate limit), 502/504 (gateway), Timeout, ConnectionError
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: retry only conditions that may go away (503, 429, network). 401 (wrong key) on retry = same wrong key. CelticWind lists retryable codes explicitly.

questions.question: What does the `with` statement guarantee?
options: Cleanup happens — `__exit__` is called even if the body raises an exception during execution
Performance — Python optimizes `with` blocks by inlining the context manager's methods
Concurrency — `with` blocks run in their own thread for isolation from the main thread
Encryption — `with` blocks encrypt any file opened inside them with a per-block random key
questions.correctAnswer: Cleanup happens — `__exit__` is called even if the body raises an exception during execution
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: with f = open(...) → f.close() always runs even on exceptions. Equivalent to try/finally but more readable. CelticWind wraps every pipeline run in pipeline_run.

questions.question: What is the role of the `__main__` block in Lesson 10's resilient pipeline?
options: Distinguishes exit codes — 0 success, 1 known CelticWindError, 2 unexpected error (code bug)
Distinguishes runtimes — runs the pipeline in dev/staging/prod based on environment variables only
Distinguishes engineers — only the on-call engineer's user ID is allowed to invoke the pipeline
Distinguishes file formats — the entry point converts CSV to Parquet before any logic runs
questions.correctAnswer: Distinguishes exit codes — 0 success, 1 known CelticWindError, 2 unexpected error (code bug)
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: sys.exit(0/1/2) communicates outcome to the orchestrator. Airflow/cron use the exit code to decide retry/alert behavior. Module 8 builds on this with validation framework.

questions.question: What is the role of Silver between Bronze and Gold?
options: Quality gate — validate, clean, type every record; rejects go to quarantine, valid records continue
Aggregation — Silver computes the regional totals that Gold reports to the billing team
Encryption — Silver encrypts Bronze data at rest; Gold contains the unencrypted business outputs
Deduplication only — Silver removes duplicate rows; Gold handles all other validation steps
questions.correctAnswer: Quality gate — validate, clean, type every record; rejects go to quarantine, valid records continue
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: Silver is the quality gate. Bronze = as received; Silver = validated/cleaned/typed. Module 8 builds the gate.

questions.question: What does schema validation catch that record-level validation does not?
options: Source system rename/add/remove of columns — caught immediately at the schema check, not deep in transforms
Single-record validation rules (negative kW, missing meter_id) — schema check is the only place these appear
Performance regressions — schema check measures DAG parsing time and alerts on slowdowns
Network outages — schema check pings the source system to verify connectivity before reading
questions.correctAnswer: Source system rename/add/remove of columns — caught immediately at the schema check, not deep in transforms
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: a renamed column is a valid CSV — just different. Schema validation catches the mismatch at the boundary instead of as a confusing KeyError later.

questions.question: What does a `coerce_*` function combine that pure validation does not?
options: Validates AND transforms in one operation — check and conversion cannot get out of sync
Validates AND logs in one operation — every check produces a structured audit log entry automatically
Validates AND retries in one operation — failed coercions retry up to 3 times before raising
Validates AND caches in one operation — successful coercions cache the result for repeated rows
questions.correctAnswer: Validates AND transforms in one operation — check and conversion cannot get out of sync
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: 'I checked the value' and 'I converted the value' are the same step. Eliminates the gap where a checker says OK but the converter fails.

questions.question: Why is `max_daily_kw=5000` documented in the code at CelticWind?
options: Business reason: 5 kW nameplate × 1000 daily conversion factor — without docs, looks like a guess
Performance reason: 5000 fits in a single CPU register — without docs, looks like an arbitrary cap
Security reason: 5000 is a Lithuanian regulatory limit — without docs, the regulator may reject the pipeline
Compatibility reason: 5000 matches the source system's column type — without docs, the source may overflow
questions.correctAnswer: Business reason: 5 kW nameplate × 1000 daily conversion factor — without docs, looks like a guess
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: every constraint has a comment explaining the business reason. Module 4's docstring discipline applied to validation rules.

questions.question: What pattern handles both CSV's empty strings and JSON's `None` for missing values?
options: raw.get(key, '').strip() or default — covers missing key, empty string, and whitespace uniformly
raw[key] if raw[key] else default — raises KeyError on missing keys, defeating the safety check
raw.get(key) or 'default' — fails when the value is the literal string '0' or False boolean
Use a third-party library that auto-detects the source format and applies the right null check
questions.correctAnswer: raw.get(key, '').strip() or default — covers missing key, empty string, and whitespace uniformly
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: CSV missing = '', JSON null = None. raw.get(key, '').strip() handles both: missing key returns '', strip handles whitespace, or applies default.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind's deduplication function return both unique records AND the count of removed duplicates?
options: Duplicates_removed becomes a structured metric — sudden spike (10K) indicates a source system problem
The count is required for billing — Apache Iceberg charges per row written to the data lake
The count is encrypted — the encryption key derives from the duplicate count for each batch
The count is needed for retry — failed deduplications retry until the count matches expectations
questions.correctAnswer: Duplicates_removed becomes a structured metric — sudden spike (10K) indicates a source system problem
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: silent removal hides problems. Logging duplicates_removed=200 lets monitoring detect 50× spikes that indicate source bugs.

questions.question: What does Pydantic give you that hand-written validators do not?
options: Class is both validation AND schema documentation — change rules in one place, all callers updated
Class is automatically tested — pytest generates test cases for every field type defined on the model
Class is multi-threaded — Pydantic validates fields in parallel across multiple CPU cores
Class is encrypted — Pydantic stores model instances in encrypted memory regions automatically
questions.correctAnswer: Class is both validation AND schema documentation — change rules in one place, all callers updated
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: MeterReadingModel = validation logic + schema documentation. Update one class when rules change. ValidationError aggregates all field errors.

questions.question: What is a 'quarantine' record at CelticWind?
options: Rejected record + rejection reasons + timestamp + source row — diagnostic evidence, not production data
Encrypted production record with stricter access control — for sensitive data subject to GDPR
Backup of the production record before transformation — for rollback if the transform is wrong
Cached copy of the production record kept for 24 hours — for repeat queries to avoid re-reading
questions.correctAnswer: Rejected record + rejection reasons + timestamp + source row — diagnostic evidence, not production data
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: quarantine = evidence of data problems. Team investigates, fixes upstream, optionally reprocesses. Quarantine files older than 90 days archive at CelticWind.

questions.question: Why does the validation report include `rejection_breakdown` (rejections by field)?
options: Trends are more informative than counts — daily rate stable? rising? Module 9's monitoring uses these
Breakdown is required by Lithuanian regulators for any quarterly compliance report submission
Breakdown is faster to compute than the total count when the dataset has more than a million rows
questions.correctAnswer: Trends are more informative than counts — daily rate stable? rising? Module 9's monitoring uses these
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: a single day shows quality; a week shows trend; a month reveals patterns (winter storms = more rejections). Reports stored as dated JSON files for the dashboard.

questions.question: What is the order of operations in CelticWind's validation framework pipeline?
options: Read → Schema check → Deduplicate → Pydantic validate → Write Silver + Quarantine → Report
Read → Pydantic validate → Schema check → Deduplicate → Write Silver + Quarantine → Report
Read → Deduplicate → Schema check → Pydantic validate → Write Silver + Quarantine → Report
Read → Write Silver → Pydantic validate → Schema check → Deduplicate → Write Quarantine → Report
questions.correctAnswer: Read → Schema check → Deduplicate → Pydantic validate → Write Silver + Quarantine → Report
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: schema fails fast on structural errors. Dedup before validation = less work. Pydantic per-record. Write outputs + report. Module 9 adds APIs.

questions.question: How does the pipeline view an API differently from a CSV file?
options: Same — both are data sources. The transport differs (network vs disk); the architecture stays the same.
Different — APIs require encryption; CSV files are read in plaintext from the local file system
Different — APIs are mutable; CSV files are immutable once written to the Bronze layer always
Different — APIs run synchronously; CSV files are read asynchronously through the threading module
questions.correctAnswer: Same — both are data sources. The transport differs (network vs disk); the architecture stays the same.
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: pipeline reads API like a file (with different transport). Read → validate → transform → write. Module 5's multi-format reader extends to APIs as the 4th source.

questions.question: Why is `timeout=30` essential on every `requests.get()`?
options: Without it, the call waits forever if the server doesn't respond — pipeline hangs at 03:00 silently
Without it, requests retries indefinitely until either the network or the cosmic ray flips a bit
Without it, Python's GC cannot release the connection's memory until interpreter shutdown
Without it, the response body is buffered fully in memory before any handler is allowed to run
questions.correctAnswer: Without it, the call waits forever if the server doesn't respond — pipeline hangs at 03:00 silently
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: no timeout = hang forever. Morning engineer sees 'still running' with no progress. CelticWind: every API call has 30s timeout (configurable in YAML).

questions.question: What is the conceptual split between `params` and `headers` in `requests.get(...)`?
options: params = request content (date, market zone); headers = metadata (auth, content-type, user-agent)
params = query string; headers = cookies — both encode key-value pairs in the same way internally
params = required parameters; headers = optional — API rejects calls without all required headers
params = encrypted payload; headers = plaintext metadata — TLS encrypts only the params dict
questions.correctAnswer: params = request content (date, market zone); headers = metadata (auth, content-type, user-agent)
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: clear separation makes API code readable. params = business logic; headers = infrastructure (auth, format, identification).

questions.question: What happens when `logger.info('Calling API with key %s', api_key)` runs?
options: Writes the API key to the log file — anyone with log access can read the secret afterwards
Encrypts the API key automatically because it matches a secret pattern recognized by the logger
Hashes the API key with SHA-256 before logging because Python's logging module redacts sensitive fields
Skips the call to logger.info because the format string contains 'key' which is in a denylist
questions.correctAnswer: Writes the API key to the log file — anyone with log access can read the secret afterwards
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: secrets in code/config/logs = exposure. Module 2's rule: secrets only in env vars, never logged. Mask Authorization header in logs.

questions.question: Which of these HTTP errors should CelticWind's pipeline retry?
options: 503 (unavailable) and 429 (rate limited) — temporary, may succeed on retry. Not 401 or 400.
All errors with status code ≥ 400 — Apache HTTP standards mandate retry for any 4xx/5xx code
No errors — every retry costs API credits, so the pipeline must surface every failure to the team
Only 401 (auth) — the API may have rotated keys; retry forces a credential refresh from Vault
questions.correctAnswer: 503 (unavailable) and 429 (rate limited) — temporary, may succeed on retry. Not 401 or 400.
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: retry transient (503, 429, 502, 504, Timeout). Don't retry permanent (401 wrong key, 400 bad query, 404 missing). CelticWind lists retryable codes in YAML.

questions.question: Why does the cursor-pagination function include a `max_pages` safety limit?
options: API pagination bug (next_url loops, page_size mismatch) can cause infinite loops and bans
Python's recursion limit forbids more than 1000 paginated calls per function invocation by default
API providers charge a flat fee per call above 100 pages; max_pages caps the bill at predictable levels
The metadata database refuses to store more than 100 paginated batches in a single Bronze write
questions.correctAnswer: API pagination bug (next_url loops, page_size mismatch) can cause infinite loops and bans
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: pagination bugs (loop, off-by-one) can produce thousands of calls. max_pages is a safety net. CelticWind logs warning when limit is reached.

questions.question: What does the `Retry-After` header on a 429 response tell you?
options: The number of seconds to wait before retrying — respect it instead of guessing a delay
The number of retries remaining before the API key is revoked permanently for abuse
The number of bytes to skip in the response body when implementing client-side filtering
The number of distinct IPs the client must use to spread the load across the rate limiter
questions.correctAnswer: The number of seconds to wait before retrying — respect it instead of guessing a delay
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: server tells you the retry delay. Use it. CelticWind: time.sleep(int(response.headers.get('Retry-After', 60))) before retrying.

questions.question: What two purposes does API response caching serve at CelticWind?
options: Performance (avoid unnecessary API calls) AND data governance (preserve raw response for reprocessing)
Compression (reduce response size) AND encryption (protect sensitive data at rest in the cache)
Compliance (Lithuanian regulators require caching) AND analytics (Apache Iceberg requires caching)
Concurrency (allow parallel reads) AND consistency (ensure all consumers see the same response)
questions.correctAnswer: Performance (avoid unnecessary API calls) AND data governance (preserve raw response for reprocessing)
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: cached JSON is the API equivalent of a Bronze CSV file. Avoid duplicate calls + reprocess if transform logic changes — same as CSV Bronze files.

questions.question: What does `timeout=(5, 30)` mean in `requests.get(...)`?
options: 5 seconds to establish the connection (TCP+TLS), 30 seconds for the server to send the full response
5 retries on connect failure, 30 retries on read failure — separate counts for each kind of error
5 milliseconds for high-priority calls, 30 seconds for low-priority calls within the same request
5 (HTTP version) and 30 (default keep-alive timeout in seconds) — both are connection-level settings
questions.correctAnswer: 5 seconds to establish the connection (TCP+TLS), 30 seconds for the server to send the full response
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: connect_timeout (TCP+TLS handshake), read_timeout (full response). ConnectTimeout vs ReadTimeout — both are subclasses of Timeout, both retryable.

questions.question: What does `EpexPriceClient` encapsulate as a class?
options: Authentication, caching, rate limiting, and retry — a clean interface that the pipeline calls simply
Threading, multiprocessing, and asyncio — three concurrency models that the pipeline can choose between
Database connection, transaction management, and rollback — for atomic API-to-DB pipelines
Compilation, type checking, and serialization — Python's standard infrastructure for API clients
questions.correctAnswer: Authentication, caching, rate limiting, and retry — a clean interface that the pipeline calls simply
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: client.get_prices(date) hides auth, cache check, retry, rate limit. Pipeline gets a clean interface. Module 10 teaches mocking such clients.

questions.question: What does Module 10 close from Module 1's six gaps?
options: 'Testing is manual: I ran it and it looked right' becomes 'pytest verifies every function automatically'
'Configuration is hardcoded' becomes 'configuration lives in environment-specific YAML files'
'Imports are absolute' becomes 'imports use relative paths within the src/ directory tree'
'Errors crash the pipeline' becomes 'errors are caught silently and the pipeline continues running'
questions.correctAnswer: 'Testing is manual: I ran it and it looked right' becomes 'pytest verifies every function automatically'
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: closes the last gap from Module 1's list. After Module 10, all six gaps closed. Code = production-grade data engineering Python.

questions.question: What does CelticWind's test naming convention look like?
options: test_{function}_{behavior} — e.g., test_validate_reading_rejects_negative_kw
test_{number} — e.g., test_001, test_002 — sequential numbering for each test in a file
{function}_test — e.g., validate_reading_test — function name first followed by suffix
should_{behavior} — e.g., should_reject_negative_kw — BDD-style assertion-first naming
questions.correctAnswer: test_{function}_{behavior} — e.g., test_validate_reading_rejects_negative_kw
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: failure message says exactly what is broken. test_validate_reading_rejects_negative_kw immediately points to the behavior under test.

questions.question: Why does CelticWind use a one-to-one mapping between source modules and test modules?
options: Adding a function to validators.py = adding tests to test_validators.py — predictable test home
pytest discovers tests faster when the module names match — directory scans skip unmatched files
The Python import system requires test modules to mirror source modules for namespace correctness
Apache PEP 8 mandates this mapping for any package distributed through PyPI to the public
questions.correctAnswer: Adding a function to validators.py = adding tests to test_validators.py — predictable test home
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: predictable test location. New function in module X → tests go in test_X.py. Module 4's organizational discipline carries through to tests.

questions.question: What does pytest do with a fixture between test functions?
options: Each test function gets a fresh copy — modifying the fixture in one test does not affect others
All test functions share the same fixture instance — modifications persist across the test run
Fixtures are loaded once at the start and reset only when the entire test suite completes
Fixtures are reloaded from disk between tests — pytest re-reads the fixture file each time
questions.correctAnswer: Each test function gets a fresh copy — modifying the fixture in one test does not affect others
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: each test gets a fresh copy. Test isolation: a test that passes in isolation passes when run with others. Order does not affect results.

questions.question: When should you use `@pytest.mark.parametrize` instead of separate test functions?
options: Same assertion logic, different inputs (valid Dublin/Cork/Galway) — parametrize lists the variations
Different assertion logic, different inputs — separate test functions for each behavior tested
Same input, different output expectations — parametrize specifies multiple acceptable answers
Random inputs — parametrize generates random test data on every run automatically by default
questions.correctAnswer: Same assertion logic, different inputs (valid Dublin/Cork/Galway) — parametrize lists the variations
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: parametrize = same logic, different data. Separate tests = different logic. CelticWind: one @parametrize per behavior with all input variations.

questions.question: What does the `tmp_path` fixture provide for testing file I/O?
options: A temporary directory unique to the test, auto-cleaned afterwards — for safe file creation
A pre-populated directory with test fixtures already loaded — read-only, shared across tests
A network-mounted shared directory — for tests that span multiple machines in the CI pool
A persistent directory that survives test runs — for caching test results between invocations
questions.correctAnswer: A temporary directory unique to the test, auto-cleaned afterwards — for safe file creation
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: tmp_path = pytest's temp dir per test. Auto-cleaned. Test creates CSV/Parquet, reader function reads it back, assertion verifies content.

questions.question: Why does `@patch('celticwind.api.requests.get', return_value=fake_response)` work but patching `'requests.get'` does not?
options: Patch the import path the module under test uses — celticwind.api imported it, so patch there
Patch the originating library — requests.get is defined there, so the patch applies globally everywhere
Patch the function reference — pass the function object directly so Python knows what to replace
Patch all paths simultaneously — pytest applies the patch to every reference of the function name
questions.correctAnswer: Patch the import path the module under test uses — celticwind.api imported it, so patch there
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: most common mocking mistake. celticwind.api imports requests.get as celticwind.api.requests.get. Patch where it's used, not where it's defined.

questions.question: Why is testing error-handling code especially important?
options: Error handlers are the least-exercised code — bugs invisible during normal operation surface only on failure
Error handlers are the most-exercised code — they run more often than the happy path in production
Error handlers must comply with Lithuanian regulations — every handler is audited quarterly by VERT
Error handlers run faster than success handlers — testing them improves the pipeline's average runtime
questions.correctAnswer: Error handlers are the least-exercised code — bugs invisible during normal operation surface only on failure
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: error handler runs only on failure — least-tested, surfaces only at the worst time. Testing ensures the safety net is functional.

questions.question: What is the warning about test coverage at CelticWind?
options: High coverage with weak assertions gives false confidence — coverage is necessary but not sufficient
100% coverage is required by Apache PEP 8 — anything below 100 fails CI and blocks deployment
Coverage tools slow tests by 10× — only run coverage on the nightly CI build, not per-commit
Coverage is maximized by running tests in parallel — pytest-xdist increases coverage automatically
questions.correctAnswer: High coverage with weak assertions gives false confidence — coverage is necessary but not sufficient
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: a test that calls validate(valid_input) covers the code but doesn't verify rejection. Code review asks: 'do tests assert the right things, or just execute the code?'

questions.question: What does Lesson 10's closing message say about taking the next framework course?
options: Will feel like an extension of what you already know — every framework assumes these skills
Will require relearning Python from scratch — frameworks have their own embedded language dialects
Will be unnecessary — this course covers everything any data engineer needs throughout their career
Will be sequenced before this one — the framework courses are prerequisites for the foundations
questions.correctAnswer: Will feel like an extension of what you already know — every framework assumes these skills
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: PySpark/Airflow/dbt/Fabric all assume project structure, error handling, tests, APIs, validation. This course = the assumed foundation.

questions.question: What is the audience shift between junior-level and mid-level Python data engineering?
options: From writing code for yourself to writing code that 3 teams and 15 engineers extend and maintain
From writing pipelines in Python to writing them in Scala for performance reasons at scale
From writing scripts manually to writing them inside a no-code platform with a visual editor
From writing code for production to writing code only for prototyping and exploratory work
questions.correctAnswer: From writing code for yourself to writing code that 3 teams and 15 engineers extend and maintain
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: junior built a pipeline; mid builds the architecture pipelines plug into. Every pattern exists because someone else uses your code without understanding it.

questions.question: What does the `@runtime_checkable Protocol` pattern give you that an abstract base class does not?
options: Structural typing — any class with the right methods satisfies the protocol without explicit inheritance
Faster method dispatch — protocols use direct attribute lookup while ABCs go through the MRO chain
