questions.explanation: Lesson 3: monorepo says 'we coordinate tightly'. Polyrepo says 'teams are autonomous'. Hybrid respects 3 founding companies' different cadences.

questions.question: What metric measures the platform team's value at the Alliance?
options: Adoption rate by product teams + developer satisfaction — not platform sophistication
Lines of code in the platform SDK + number of features delivered per quarter consistently
Number of platform engineers + how senior they are — staffing alone proves platform value
Number of incidents the platform team resolves directly without escalating to product teams
questions.correctAnswer: Adoption rate by product teams + developer satisfaction — not platform sophistication
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: 80% adoption + 7/10 satisfaction = healthy. Below = pause feature work, investigate. Platform exists to serve product teams, not architectural purity.

questions.question: What is the rule for orphaned data products at the Alliance?
options: More than 2 orphaned products triggers a governance review — first audit found 14, decommissioned 8
Orphaned products are auto-deleted after 7 days of zero consumer activity in production
Orphaned products are reassigned to the platform team automatically through round-robin selection
Orphaned products must be retained forever per Lithuanian regulatory requirements globally enforced
questions.correctAnswer: More than 2 orphaned products triggers a governance review — first audit found 14, decommissioned 8
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: no owner = nobody fixes when broken. Dashboard highlights orphans. First audit: 14 orphaned, 8 decommissioned (no consumers), 6 assigned to most-dependent team.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance use sequential cross-country deployment instead of simultaneous?
options: Simultaneous would have exposed all 3 countries to a config-loader race condition; sequential caught it
Simultaneous deployment is forbidden by Lithuanian regulators governing multi-country data platforms
Simultaneous would exceed the team's PagerDuty subscription budget for cross-country alerts daily
Sequential deployment is required by Apache HTTP servers running behind Kubernetes in production
questions.correctAnswer: Simultaneous would have exposed all 3 countries to a config-loader race condition; sequential caught it
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: sequential = 4 days of insurance for 5M-meter platform. Platform 1.8 release: race condition caught in IE prod (day 1), fixed before SC rollout (day 3).

questions.question: What configuration layering does the Alliance use across environments × countries × teams?
options: base.yml → {env}.yml → {country}/country.yml → {country}/{team}.yml — 4 layers, 27 combinations
One YAML per pipeline — config layering is forbidden because precedence rules confuse teams
All settings in environment variables — YAML files are deprecated in modern Python platforms
Three separate config files (env, country, team) merged at startup with random precedence rules
questions.correctAnswer: base.yml → {env}.yml → {country}/country.yml → {country}/{team}.yml — 4 layers, 27 combinations
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: 3-dim hierarchy = 3 envs × 3 countries × 3 teams = 27 combos. CI runs 27 parametrized tests, catches errors like FR team.yml accidentally overriding country max_daily_kw.

questions.question: What does the Alliance's 'blessed dependencies' policy enforce?
options: Specific version ranges for tested dependencies — CI fails if a team's pin falls outside the blessed range
All teams must use the same exact version of every dependency without any deviation permitted
Apache-approved dependencies only — non-Apache packages are forbidden across the entire platform
Free-only dependencies — any commercial library requires platform team budget approval first
questions.correctAnswer: Specific version ranges for tested dependencies — CI fails if a team's pin falls outside the blessed range
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: French team's 0.21.0 Polars pin → null-handling incident corrupted 12,000 billing records. Now: blessed ranges, CI gate enforces compliance.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance budget 20% of each platform release cycle for migration tooling?
options: Migration speed determines adoption rate — 4-hour migration with codemods vs 3-day manual = adoption
20% is the Apache-recommended percentage for any open-source platform's migration documentation
20% is the maximum that Lithuanian regulators allow for non-feature work in any release cycle
20% leaves room for unexpected emergencies that arise during the production deployment phase
questions.correctAnswer: Migration speed determines adoption rate — 4-hour migration with codemods vs 3-day manual = adoption
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: 3-day migration = reluctant adoption. 4-hour migration with codemods = eager. Investment pays for itself in adoption speed.

questions.question: What does the Alliance Platform Blueprint reflect at the highest level?
options: Hybrid repo + 4-level namespace + producer-owned products + sequential country promotion
Pure monorepo + flat namespace + platform-owned products + simultaneous global deployment
Pure polyrepo + 2-level namespace + consumer-owned products + manual deployment approval
Cloud-native + serverless + auto-scaling + zero-touch operations across all infrastructure
questions.correctAnswer: Hybrid repo + 4-level namespace + producer-owned products + sequential country promotion
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: each decision encodes an organizational reality. Module 2 next: CPython runtime internals beyond what profilers reveal.

questions.question: What does Module 2 teach beyond what profiling tools reveal?
options: Why the runtime behaves as it does — implementation-level understanding enables fixes profiling cannot suggest
How to write Python without profiling — runtime knowledge replaces the need for any profiling tools
How to skip Module 8 mid-level — runtime internals are sufficient for all performance optimization
How to bypass the GIL — runtime knowledge allows direct memory manipulation that ignores GIL constraints
questions.correctAnswer: Why the runtime behaves as it does — implementation-level understanding enables fixes profiling cannot suggest
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: profiling = 'function takes 40s'. Runtime = 'because re.match compiles a DFA per call, allocating from pymalloc's 256-byte pool that fragments...'

questions.question: What is the diagnostic rule for distinguishing pymalloc arena retention from a memory leak?
options: Measure RSS across 5 consecutive runs — linear growth = leak; stabilization after first run = retention
Always treat RSS growth as a leak — the only fix is to restart the process every hour preemptively
Compare tracemalloc to RSS — if they differ by 10%, it is a leak in C extension memory allocators
Run gc.collect() after every chunk — if RSS does not drop, the leak is in cyclic references somewhere
questions.correctAnswer: Measure RSS across 5 consecutive runs — linear growth = leak; stabilization after first run = retention
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: 400→520→520→520 = retention (normal). 400→450→500→550→600 = leak. Arenas plateau at peak; leaks grow linearly.

questions.question: Why does CPython's reference counting work for `del large_list` but PyPy does not?
options: CPython guarantees immediate deallocation when refcount hits 0; PyPy uses a tracing GC without that guarantee
CPython is faster than PyPy at all operations including memory deallocation across data engineering workloads
PyPy is incompatible with del entirely — the keyword raises a runtime error in PyPy 3.10+ versions
CPython has a special memory allocator for lists; PyPy uses the standard system malloc which is slower
questions.correctAnswer: CPython guarantees immediate deallocation when refcount hits 0; PyPy uses a tracing GC without that guarantee
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: CPython's refcount + immediate dealloc = del frees memory now. PyPy/Jython/GraalPy use tracing GC. Pattern fails on non-CPython implementations.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance set GC thresholds to `(700, 10, 50)` for Celery workers?
options: Reduces gen-2 collection frequency 5× — pause cost goes from 50-200ms to less per pipeline run
Required by Apache standards for any Celery worker that processes more than 1000 tasks per hour
Increases memory usage 5× to fit larger data structures — performance trade-off for capacity
Disables generational GC entirely — workers run faster without any garbage collection overhead
questions.correctAnswer: Reduces gen-2 collection frequency 5× — pause cost goes from 50-200ms to less per pipeline run
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: default (700, 10, 10) = gen-2 every 70K allocs. (700, 10, 50) = every 350K. Fewer pauses for long-lived workers.

questions.question: What is the accurate rule about threading vs multiprocessing in Python?
options: 'Use threading for any operation that releases the GIL' — not just I/O — including pyarrow, Polars, numpy
'Use threading for I/O, multiprocessing for CPU' — the simple rule is sufficient for all workloads
'Use threading for everything in Python 3.13+' — the GIL was removed entirely in that version
'Use multiprocessing for everything' — threading has too many bugs for production data engineering work
questions.correctAnswer: 'Use threading for any operation that releases the GIL' — not just I/O — including pyarrow, Polars, numpy
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: GIL released by pq.read_table, pl.filter, np.dot, file I/O. GIL held by re.match, dict construction, float(). Map each step to its GIL behavior.

questions.question: When is a Cython or Rust extension justified at the Alliance?
options: Profiled bottleneck + ≥5× speedup potential + stable interface (not modified frequently)
Always — pure Python is too slow for any production data engineering pipeline regardless of scale
Never — Apache PEP 8 forbids non-Python code in any data platform for portability reasons
When the team has Rust expertise — performance gain is secondary to engineer skill alignment
questions.correctAnswer: Profiled bottleneck + ≥5× speedup potential + stable interface (not modified frequently)
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: pure Python = debuggable, testable, readable. Cython needs C compiler, Rust needs toolchain. Justify with profile + speedup + stability.

questions.question: What is the resource management requirement for `multiprocessing.shared_memory`?
options: Every segment must be unlinked on exit (incl. exceptions) — orphans consume memory indefinitely
Every segment must be encrypted with a per-segment key from Vault before any worker can access it
Every segment must be replicated across all workers to ensure availability during process failures
Every segment is auto-released when the parent process exits — manual cleanup is not needed
questions.correctAnswer: Every segment must be unlinked on exit (incl. exceptions) — orphans consume memory indefinitely
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: shared memory persists until explicitly unlinked. Crashed worker = orphaned segment forever. Context manager + periodic cleanup of segments > 1 hour old.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance use lazy imports for pyarrow/Polars/pydantic?
options: Eager import = 2080ms startup; lazy = 80ms — fits Kubernetes liveness probe timeouts cleanly
Lazy imports are 5× faster than eager imports because Python optimizes deferred imports through caching
Eager imports are forbidden by Apache PEP 8 for any package that is larger than 100 KB on disk
Lazy imports automatically decrypt encrypted modules when they are first accessed by the worker
questions.correctAnswer: Eager import = 2080ms startup; lazy = 80ms — fits Kubernetes liveness probe timeouts cleanly
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: pyarrow 800ms, polars 600ms, pydantic 400ms. Lazy = workers start within probe timeouts. -X importtime profiles transitive imports.

questions.question: What is the runtime-level optimization decision criterion?
options: Not 'which is faster?' but 'which is fast enough AND maintainable by the team we have?'
Always 'which is faster?' — performance is the only metric that matters in data engineering at scale
Always 'which has the best documentation?' — engineer onboarding outweighs all performance concerns
Always 'which is most popular?' — Apache-recommended tools must be used regardless of fit
questions.correctAnswer: Not 'which is faster?' but 'which is fast enough AND maintainable by the team we have?'
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: Rust 0.8s impressive, Python 33s maintainable. SLA needs sub-5s = Rust justified. SLA allows 60s = Python better engineering choice.

questions.question: How does Module 3 build on Module 2's runtime knowledge?
options: 5M meters exceeds single 8-core server even with optimizations — distribute across worker fleet
5M meters require rewriting Python in Rust — Module 2 was preparation for the language switch
5M meters need a custom Python interpreter — Module 3 introduces a forked CPython with no GIL
5M meters fit on one server with the right runtime tuning — Module 3 covers single-server scaling
questions.correctAnswer: 5M meters exceeds single 8-core server even with optimizations — distribute across worker fleet
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: GIL map (M2) determines threading vs distributing within a worker. Shared memory (M2.7) for zero-copy intra-server. Module 3 = horizontal scaling.

questions.question: What is the operational cost of distribution that Lesson 1 emphasizes?
options: Single-server has 1 failure mode; distributed has 15-20 across broker, workers, results, network
Single-server is forbidden at scale — Apache requires distributed architectures for all platforms
Distributed is always faster — single-server is 10× slower than any distributed alternative
Distributed costs 50% more in cloud bills — cost is the only meaningful trade-off to consider
questions.correctAnswer: Single-server has 1 failure mode; distributed has 15-20 across broker, workers, results, network
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: every lesson addresses both the pattern AND its failure modes because operational complexity is the cost of distribution.

questions.question: What is the rule for Celery task arguments at the Alliance?
options: Pass file paths and config keys, not data — task loads fresh data inside the worker for idempotency
Pass Pydantic models directly — Celery serializes them automatically through pickle protocol 5
Pass Polars DataFrames — Celery's columnar serializer handles them more efficiently than dicts
Pass file handles — Celery shares file descriptors across worker processes through fork inheritance
questions.correctAnswer: Pass file paths and config keys, not data — task loads fresh data inside the worker for idempotency
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: Polars DF / Pydantic model / file handle = serialization fail. Path + config keys are JSON-serializable. Worker re-reads data = idempotent.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance use 7 queues instead of 25 sub-region queues?
options: Single, unambiguous purpose per queue — 7 queue depth charts vs 25, no cross-region routing confusion
Apache Celery limits queue count to 7 in the open-source build — the enterprise edition supports more
Lithuanian regulations cap queue counts at 7 for any data platform serving cross-border workloads
Redis broker performance degrades significantly above 8 queues — 7 is the optimal number empirically
questions.correctAnswer: Single, unambiguous purpose per queue — 7 queue depth charts vs 25, no cross-region routing confusion
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: 25 queues = monitoring complexity + routing confusion. 7 queues (4 country + priority + high-mem + DLQ) handle all sub-regions within country queues.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance defer pyarrow imports until first task execution in Celery prefork workers?
options: Reduces parent memory 400MB → 80MB; avoids copy-on-write duplication of pyarrow's C++ allocator state
Required by Celery for any worker pool size above 4 — pyarrow conflicts with prefork below that threshold
Apache PEP 8 forbids importing pyarrow before fork() in any production data engineering platform
Lazy import is the only way Celery can detect when pyarrow is actually needed by a specific task
questions.correctAnswer: Reduces parent memory 400MB → 80MB; avoids copy-on-write duplication of pyarrow's C++ allocator state
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: prefork inherits parent's memory pages (CoW). Heavy import in parent = each child duplicates pages on first write. Lazy import in child = no duplication.

questions.question: Why must Celery chord header tasks return lightweight results?
options: 365 × 10MB results = 3.6GB Redis memory before callback runs — chord header writes to storage, returns path
Apache Celery has a 1 KB hard limit on chord header results — larger payloads are silently truncated
Lightweight results are 10× faster — chord performance scales linearly with header result size only
Lithuanian regulators forbid chord header results above 1 KB for any production data platform always
questions.correctAnswer: 365 × 10MB results = 3.6GB Redis memory before callback runs — chord header writes to storage, returns path
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: chord stores all header results in Redis until callback. Large results = OOM. Header writes to S3, returns path. Caps Redis at ~1KB per task.

questions.question: What does the Alliance do with the dead letter queue every day at 08:00?
options: Review each task — resubmit with fix, escalate if systemic, or acknowledge if expected and rerunnable
Auto-reprocess every task — DLQ entries are retried infinitely until they finally succeed every time
Auto-delete every task — DLQ is purged daily to prevent memory bloat and Redis cost growth
Forward to Apache Foundation — Apache reviews failed tasks for the open-source community catalog
questions.correctAnswer: Review each task — resubmit with fix, escalate if systemic, or acknowledge if expected and rerunnable
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: DLQ task = small incident (data not processed). > 10 in queue triggers incident response. Daily review classifies and acts on each.

questions.question: What is the rule for Celery task results at the Alliance?
options: Lightweight results in Redis (TTL 1h); large outputs to S3 + return path; ignore_result=True for fire-and-forget
All results in Redis with no TTL — durability is more important than memory efficiency in production
All results in PostgreSQL — Apache standards require relational storage for any production task results
No results stored anywhere — fire-and-forget is the default for every task type in production
questions.correctAnswer: Lightweight results in Redis (TTL 1h); large outputs to S3 + return path; ignore_result=True for fire-and-forget
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: 50MB result × N tasks = unsustainable Redis memory. Pattern: lightweight Redis result (path/count/status) + heavy output to S3.

questions.question: How does OpenTelemetry's trace_id extend Module 9 mid-level's run_id?
options: run_id = single process; trace_id = across multiple processes/servers via Celery headers + Jaeger
trace_id replaces run_id entirely — OpenTelemetry deprecated run_id in version 1.20 of the spec
trace_id is encrypted but run_id is not — security through obscurity for distributed pipeline tracing
trace_id requires a paid OpenTelemetry license but run_id is free — only paid platforms scale globally
questions.correctAnswer: run_id = single process; trace_id = across multiple processes/servers via Celery headers + Jaeger
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: same engineering pattern (bind, propagate, correlate). Different scope: structlog contextvars (call stack) vs Celery headers + collector (multi-process).

questions.question: What is the Alliance's threshold for distributing a pipeline?
options: Runtime > 80% of SLA on the target server — below that, sequential local execution is faster overall
Always distribute — Apache standards require distributed architecture for any production pipeline
Never distribute — single-server is always faster after considering serialization and broker overhead
Distribute when the team has 5+ engineers — staffing determines architecture independent of workload
questions.correctAnswer: Runtime > 80% of SLA on the target server — below that, sequential local execution is faster overall
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: 3-pipeline rejected proposals saved 60h migration + ongoing operational overhead. 4.5s distributed > 3s local with 4× failure modes = no distribution.

questions.question: How does Module 4 build on Module 3's distributed task architecture?
options: Module 4's GraphQL data product API submits Celery tasks via Module 3 — execution layer behind the consumption interface
Module 4 replaces Module 3 — APIs are the modern alternative to distributed task processing entirely
Module 4 is independent of Module 3 — they cover unrelated platform capabilities at the same scale
Module 4 wraps Module 3 — GraphQL becomes the only way to invoke Celery tasks at the Alliance
questions.correctAnswer: Module 4's GraphQL data product API submits Celery tasks via Module 3 — execution layer behind the consumption interface
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: GraphQL query that triggers data product refresh = Celery task to country queue. Module 4 builds consumption interface; Module 3 is execution engine.

questions.question: What does the API contract enable that direct file consumption does not?
options: Producer can change storage format/engine/schedule without affecting consumers — decoupling at the contract surface
Consumer can write data back to the producer — APIs enable bidirectional file modification semantics
Producer can encrypt data with consumer-specific keys — APIs are the only way to do per-consumer crypto
Consumer can run arbitrary SQL — APIs delegate query execution back to the consumer's database
questions.correctAnswer: Producer can change storage format/engine/schedule without affecting consumers — decoupling at the contract surface
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: file consumption = consumer knows path, schema, partitions, schedule. API = stable contract; storage format change (Parquet→Delta) invisible to consumers.

questions.question: What does the Alliance enforce on every GraphQL endpoint?
options: Max query depth 3, max field count 50, query cost budget 1000 points
Max query depth 100, max field count 10000, no cost budget — let consumers query freely
Max query depth 1, max field count 5, no nested relations — only flat queries are permitted
Apache GraphQL standard limits — every platform uses the same depth and field count limits globally
questions.correctAnswer: Max query depth 3, max field count 50, query cost budget 1000 points
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: GraphQL nesting = expensive joins. Depth 3 + 50 fields + 1000-point budget prevent runaway queries. 400 with limit-exceeded message.

questions.question: What does a webhook endpoint need to do within the source system's timeout?
options: Validate, deduplicate, queue to Celery in ~50ms — return 200 before any actual processing begins
Process the entire event synchronously — return 200 only after the event is fully written to storage
Forward the event to Kafka without any validation — defer all processing to downstream consumers
Encrypt the event with a per-source key — return 200 only after the encryption key is rotated
questions.correctAnswer: Validate, deduplicate, queue to Celery in ~50ms — return 200 before any actual processing begins
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: 5-10s source timeout. Sync 15s processing = source times out, retries, creates duplicates. Validate + dedupe + queue + return 200 in 50ms.

questions.question: What is the per-team connection limit on the Alliance's SSE endpoint?
options: 5 simultaneous connections — beyond limit returns 429; tracked as Prometheus gauge
Unlimited — SSE is a stateless protocol so connection counts have no practical meaning at scale
1 connection — only one SSE consumer per team is permitted to prevent any kind of fanout
100 connections — generous limit that no team has ever come close to reaching in production
questions.correctAnswer: 5 simultaneous connections — beyond limit returns 429; tracked as Prometheus gauge
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: each SSE connection holds server thread + subscription. 1000-connection client = exhaust server. Limit + 429 + dashboard.

questions.question: Who owns the data product versioning process at the Alliance?
options: The data product's owner team — they communicate, write migration guide, track adoption
The platform team — they handle every product's versioning to ensure consistency across the alliance
The consumers — they decide when each version is sunset and force the producer to comply
The Lithuanian regulators — they dictate versioning timelines for all production data platforms
questions.correctAnswer: The data product's owner team — they communicate, write migration guide, track adoption
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: URL versioning is trivial. Hard part = org process. Owner manages migration. Platform provides tooling (versioned routing, deprecation headers, dashboard).

questions.question: How does the Alliance treat 403 responses?
options: Security signal — logged with team/scope/timestamp; > 10 denials in 24h triggers automated investigation
Standard error — logged at debug level only and never reviewed by the security team afterwards
Auto-grant — 403 responses trigger automatic permission grants to keep teams unblocked at all times
Customer-facing message — 403 responses are translated into user-friendly text in three languages
questions.correctAnswer: Security signal — logged with team/scope/timestamp; > 10 denials in 24h triggers automated investigation
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: 403 may indicate misconfigured client, credential compromise, or unauthorized attempt. Weekly security review of denial patterns.

questions.question: What headers does the Alliance include on every API response for rate limiting?
options: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset — for adaptive client throttling
X-RateLimit-IP — only per-source IP tracking, no team or token information exposed for privacy
X-RateLimit-Cost — only the cost of the current query, no budget or remaining capacity exposed
No headers — clients should retry on 429 without any additional rate-limiting metadata
questions.correctAnswer: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset — for adaptive client throttling
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: 800/1000 consumed = client slows proactively. Self-regulating clients never hit the limit. Three headers enable adaptive throttling.

questions.question: Why does every cached API response include `X-Cache-Age`?
options: Consumer can detect stale data (X-Cache-Age: 86400 = 24h old) and decide whether to bypass cache
Required by Apache HTTP standards for any cached API response in production environments
Performance optimization — consumers cache responses locally based on the X-Cache-Age value alone
Lithuanian regulators require cache-age headers on every API response in production data platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Consumer can detect stale data (X-Cache-Age: 86400 = 24h old) and decide whether to bypass cache
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: cache that is never invalidated serves stale data forever with 200 OK. X-Cache-Age + Cache-Control: no-cache bypass enable consumer awareness.

questions.question: When does building a data product API NOT make sense?
options: Single team consumer on the same cluster — over-engineering vs direct file access
More than 3 consumers — APIs are required by Apache standards for any multi-consumer dataset
Cross-cluster consumer — direct file access works fine across cluster boundaries with NFS mounts
Field-level authorization needed — APIs cannot do field-level permissions at production scale
questions.correctAnswer: Single team consumer on the same cluster — over-engineering vs direct file access
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: API valuable when consumer base grows (caching amortizes), consumers remote (abstracts location), access control exceeds filesystem. Build when justified.

questions.question: What is the relationship between Module 4's GraphQL schema and Module 5's Protobuf schema?
options: GraphQL = API surface; Protobuf = data contract underneath; rename detected by registry before deploy
GraphQL replaces Protobuf — modern data products use GraphQL exclusively and have abandoned Protobuf
Protobuf wraps GraphQL — the API serves Protobuf binary responses with GraphQL as an internal index
Both are equivalent — choose one based on team preference for the API serialization format
questions.correctAnswer: GraphQL = API surface; Protobuf = data contract underneath; rename detected by registry before deploy
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: M4 governs consumption interface, M5 governs data contract. Together = complete data product governance stack. M4 versioning + M5 schema registry.

questions.question: Why does Lesson 1 say schemas have both technical and organizational components?
options: Tools detect breaks (registry, compatibility checker, CI); organization coordinates evolution (90-day windows)
Tools alone are sufficient — Apache schema registries handle every aspect of schema evolution automatically
Organization alone is sufficient — well-coordinated teams do not need any technical schema enforcement
Schemas are purely technical — organizational coordination has no role in schema evolution at all
questions.correctAnswer: Tools detect breaks (registry, compatibility checker, CI); organization coordinates evolution (90-day windows)
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: technical components straightforward. Organizational components require cross-team coordination tools alone cannot provide. Module covers both.

questions.question: What is the absolute rule about Protobuf field numbers at the Alliance?
options: Reserved field numbers can never be reused — old consumers deserialize new bytes as old type, garbage value silently
Field numbers can be reused after 30 days — Apache Protobuf relaxes this rule for production schemas
Field numbers must increase monotonically — gaps indicate breaking changes that cannot be undone
Field numbers must match column ordering in the storage format — Parquet column index must equal field number
questions.correctAnswer: Reserved field numbers can never be reused — old consumers deserialize new bytes as old type, garbage value silently
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: field 100 was raw_kw (float64). Reused as source_system (string) = old consumers deserialize string bytes as float = garbage. Reserved at .proto + CI gate enforce.

questions.question: What does the Alliance's pipeline do before writing data?
options: Calls registry.register(schema) — write blocked if registration fails; prevents data without registered schema
Calls registry.fetch(latest) — uses whatever is registered regardless of compatibility with new data
Skips registration — pipelines write freely and consumers fetch the latest schema each time they read
Calls registry.delete_old() — clears old versions to keep the registry small and fast across queries
questions.correctAnswer: Calls registry.register(schema) — write blocked if registration fails; prevents data without registered schema
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: register before write. Failed registration (incompatible) = pipeline exits without writing. Prevents 'data exists that no schema describes' scenario.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance use FULL compatibility (not BACKWARD or FORWARD)?
options: Cross-country promotion window (5 days) makes version mismatches inevitable; only FULL handles both directions
FULL is required by Apache for any production schema registry — BACKWARD/FORWARD are deprecated modes
FULL is faster than BACKWARD — the compatibility check completes in half the time on average
FULL allows breaking changes that BACKWARD prohibits — flexibility is more valuable than safety
questions.correctAnswer: Cross-country promotion window (5 days) makes version mismatches inevitable; only FULL handles both directions
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: FULL prohibits removing any field (even optional). Cross-country mismatch window = need both old-and-new and new-and-old reads. Cleanup deferred 1-2 quarters.

questions.question: What does the Alliance do about CI gate bypass for schema compatibility?
options: Bypass requires platform team approval via signed commit; unauthorized bypass triggers security alert
Bypass is freely allowed — engineers self-certify when they need to ship a breaking change quickly
Bypass is impossible — the gate cannot be disabled by anyone for any reason at any time
Bypass requires Lithuanian regulator approval — the regulator reviews every bypass request individually
questions.correctAnswer: Bypass requires platform team approval via signed commit; unauthorized bypass triggers security alert
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: NONE compatibility 'temporarily' set + forgotten = 3 subsequent breaking changes shipped undetected. Now signed commits + security alerts.

questions.question: What does sample data exercise that schema validation does not?
options: Format conventions: meter_id is 14-char IE-DUB-N-00142, optional fields both present/absent, boundary values
Performance characteristics — sample data times the validator's throughput on representative loads
Encryption strength — sample data tests the schema registry's TLS configuration for production use
Replication consistency — sample data verifies that the schema registry replicates correctly across regions
questions.correctAnswer: Format conventions: meter_id is 14-char IE-DUB-N-00142, optional fields both present/absent, boundary values
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: schema says 'meter_id is string'. Sample says 'looks like IE-DUB-N-00142'. Consumer parses format, depends on it. Sample exercises range of values producer may produce.

questions.question: When should you use the parallel-field strategy for schema evolution?
options: Renaming a field — keep old name during migration, all consumers use new name, then deprecate old
Adding a field — adding always uses parallel-field, never additive, regardless of risk profile
Removing a field — removing requires parallel-field to give consumers time to migrate away
Changing a type — all type changes use parallel-field with both fields present simultaneously
questions.correctAnswer: Renaming a field — keep old name during migration, all consumers use new name, then deprecate old
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: rename (daily_kw → consumption_kw): both fields present, consumers migrate to new name, old deprecated. Cost = double storage during migration.

questions.question: Where do business rules belong vs where do schema definitions belong?
options: Schema = types + optionality. Contract layer = validation rules (regex, ranges, conditionals)
Schema = everything including business rules. Apache Protobuf supports conditional required fields
Schema = only field names. Contract layer handles types, optionality, and business rules together
Schema = only field IDs. Everything else (names, types, rules) is in the contract layer
questions.correctAnswer: Schema = types + optionality. Contract layer = validation rules (regex, ranges, conditionals)
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: schema with conditional required ('required if country=IE') becomes a programming language. Schema = types/optionality. Contract = Pydantic + validation rules.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance NOT register every team-internal schema?
options: 300+ hours/year governance overhead with no cross-team benefit — registry overwhelmed, platform team consumed
Apache PEP 8 forbids registering team-internal schemas in any production schema registry globally
Team-internal schemas evolve too quickly — registry cannot keep up with the daily rate of changes
Team-internal schemas are confidential — registering them in a shared registry leaks team-specific knowledge
questions.correctAnswer: 300+ hours/year governance overhead with no cross-team benefit — registry overwhelmed, platform team consumed
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: 200 subjects, platform team 40% on internal reviews, teams resented bureaucracy. Reduced to cross-team only = freed both for governance that prevents incidents.

questions.question: How does Module 6 build on Module 5's schema definitions?
options: AST-based generator translates Protobuf schemas to Pydantic models automatically — eliminates manual sync at 45 schemas
Module 6 replaces Module 5's Protobuf with Pydantic entirely — Pydantic is the modern data engineering standard
Module 6 forks Module 5's registry into a Pydantic-only system — Protobuf is deprecated for new platforms
Module 6 ignores Module 5 — metaprogramming has no relationship with schema governance ever
questions.correctAnswer: AST-based generator translates Protobuf schemas to Pydantic models automatically — eliminates manual sync at 45 schemas
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: 45 cross-team schemas × manual Pydantic sync = hours per quarter. Generator eliminates the manual step. Schema governance defines, M6 machinery turns into Python code.

questions.question: How is the platform SDK's API quality measured?
options: Discoverability + correctness + diagnostics + stability — same standards as any public API
Test coverage + documentation pages + open issues — quantitative metrics from the codebase
Lines of code + number of features + release cadence — productivity metrics from the team
Adoption rate + uptime + cost — business metrics from the platform's customers downstream
questions.correctAnswer: Discoverability + correctness + diagnostics + stability — same standards as any public API
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: SDK users are engineers writing Python code. API quality measured by same standards as public APIs. Metaprogramming serves these qualities.

questions.question: When are descriptors the right tool vs `__init_subclass__`?
options: Descriptors for individual attribute behavior; __init_subclass__ for class-level behavior
Descriptors are deprecated in Python 3.13 — __init_subclass__ replaces all use cases for descriptors
Always descriptors — they provide more flexibility than __init_subclass__ in every scenario
Always __init_subclass__ — descriptors are too low-level for any modern Python platform code
questions.correctAnswer: Descriptors for individual attribute behavior; __init_subclass__ for class-level behavior
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: ConfigField (per-attribute validation) = descriptor. AlliancePipeline registration (class-level) = __init_subclass__. Use simplest tool for the problem.

questions.question: What is the 4:1 ratio in the Alliance SDK?
options: 4 base classes use __init_subclass__; 1 uses metaclass — simpler tool when sufficient
4 metaclasses; 1 __init_subclass__ — Apache standards prefer metaclasses for most platforms
4 descriptors; 1 metaclass — descriptors handle most cases at the Alliance's metaprogramming layer
4:1 is a code coverage ratio — 4 lines of test for every 1 line of metaprogramming code
questions.correctAnswer: 4 base classes use __init_subclass__; 1 uses metaclass — simpler tool when sufficient
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: AlliancePipeline, AllianceTask, AllianceDataProduct, AllianceSchema use __init_subclass__. SchemaModelMeta is the only metaclass. Reflects 'simplest tool' principle.

questions.question: What is the 'surprise test' principle for metaprogramming?
options: Engineer defines subclass, asserts on every attribute — undeclared/undocumented attribute = surprise to eliminate
Engineer is surprised by performance gains — speedup must exceed 5× to justify metaprogramming complexity
Engineer is surprised by error messages — error message clarity is the only criterion for metaclass quality
Engineer is surprised by API ergonomics — fluent builder vs decorator API choice depends on team taste
questions.correctAnswer: Engineer defines subclass, asserts on every attribute — undeclared/undocumented attribute = surprise to eliminate
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: metaclass that adds unexpected attributes/methods/error messages = unnecessary cognitive load. Surprise tests catch this and demand removal.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance use file-based plugin discovery for some pipelines?
options: 200-file scan = 1.2s startup. Acceptable for long-lived workers (Celery), prohibitive for CLI tools
File-based is faster than entry points — Apache standards recommend file-based for all production plugins
Entry points are deprecated — Apache removed entry-point support in importlib.metadata 5.0+ versions
File-based is more secure — entry points expose the package to dependency injection attacks at runtime
questions.correctAnswer: 200-file scan = 1.2s startup. Acceptable for long-lived workers (Celery), prohibitive for CLI tools
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: 200 files × import = 1.2s. Workers start once, run hours = OK. CLI tool per invocation = prohibitive. CLI uses explicit registration.

questions.question: What is the rule about generated code at the Alliance?
options: Commit the generated code; CI verifies committed matches generator output — mismatch fails build
Generate at runtime always — committed generated code drifts from source and creates inconsistencies
Never generate — Apache standards forbid code generation in any production data platform globally
Generate only in production — staging and dev environments use hand-written equivalents instead
questions.correctAnswer: Commit the generated code; CI verifies committed matches generator output — mismatch fails build
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: runtime gen = depends on generator + .proto access + startup timeout. Committed gen = static file, normal import, reviewable in PR. CI matches committed to gen output.

questions.question: What does every Alliance SDK error message follow?
options: Pattern: what is wrong + which object + how to fix — error messages teach the API while resolving
Pattern: stack trace + module name + line number — full Python diagnostic without any fix suggestion
Pattern: exception class + numeric code + lookup URL — engineers consult external docs for resolution
Pattern: random encouragement + bug emoji + jocular tone — keeps debugging sessions psychologically light
questions.correctAnswer: Pattern: what is wrong + which object + how to fix — error messages teach the API while resolving
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: 'Pipeline ie.billing.meters has no write step. Add .write(...) before .build()'. What/which/how. Tested in SDK test suite alongside positive behavior.

questions.question: What does the Alliance test suite for metaprogramming NOT do?
options: Never imports the metaclass directly — tests user experience (defining a subclass, asserting on visible attributes)
Never uses pytest fixtures — fixtures and metaclasses are incompatible across Python implementations
Never tests positive paths — only negative paths to catch the metaclass's failure modes systematically
Never runs in CI — metaclass tests are too slow for the per-PR test budget at the Alliance
questions.correctAnswer: Never imports the metaclass directly — tests user experience (defining a subclass, asserting on visible attributes)
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: testing internal state = testing implementation. Testing visible attributes = testing user experience. Tests break when UX changes, not when implementation refactors.

questions.question: What is the fallback documentation requirement at the Alliance?
options: Every metaprogramming feature has documented fallback: 'If X removed, replace with manual Y, takes 11h'
Every metaprogramming feature has Apache certification — fallbacks are unnecessary with proper certification
Every metaprogramming feature has runtime version detection — falls back automatically across Python versions
Every metaprogramming feature has been tested by Lithuanian regulators — fallbacks are required by law
questions.correctAnswer: Every metaprogramming feature has documented fallback: 'If X removed, replace with manual Y, takes 11h'
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: 5 platform engineers leave = 45 inherit code they may not understand. Fallback docs make metaprogramming an optimization, not a dependency that cannot be unwound.

questions.question: How does Module 7 connect to Module 6's metaprogramming?
options: Schema definitions (M5) determine Parquet column types; generated Pydantic models (M6) validate before storage layer
M7 replaces M6 — storage architecture is more important than metaprogramming at platform scale
M7 is independent of M6 — they cover unrelated platform capabilities at the senior level
M7 deprecates M6 — Parquet eliminates the need for any framework engineering or metaprogramming
questions.correctAnswer: Schema definitions (M5) determine Parquet column types; generated Pydantic models (M6) validate before storage layer
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: storage architecture decisions at 18 TB. Encoding, page layout, Delta vs Iceberg, tiering. M5 schemas → Parquet types. M6 generated models → validate before storage.

questions.question: Why does the storage architecture serve more than just batch writes?
options: Storage serves Module 4 GraphQL reads, Module 5 schemas, Module 3 Celery writes, Module 9 streams
Storage architecture is independent of all other concerns — it serves only the team that writes the data
Storage architecture is purely a cost decision — performance and access patterns are secondary concerns
Storage architecture is dictated by the cloud vendor — engineers must accept their default configuration
questions.correctAnswer: Storage serves Module 4 GraphQL reads, Module 5 schemas, Module 3 Celery writes, Module 9 streams
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: optimization for batch that degrades point-lookup affects every API consumer. Storage must serve all access patterns, not just the writing pipeline.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance sort Silver meter data by `(reading_date, region)`?
options: Tight min/max statistics enable predicate pushdown — region filter reads 4 pages instead of 36
Apache Parquet requires column ordering by data type — date and region come first by convention
Sorting reduces file size by 40% — sorted data compresses better than unsorted in all cases
Sorting is required for Parquet to be readable by Spark and Trino — unsorted Parquet files break queries
questions.correctAnswer: Tight min/max statistics enable predicate pushdown — region filter reads 4 pages instead of 36
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: each page has min/max stats. Sort by query columns concentrates region's data in few pages. Higher-cardinality first (date), then lower (region).

questions.question: What is the Parquet tuning methodology at the Alliance?
options: Measure I/O with default → change one parameter → re-measure → compare. Never tune by intuition
Always max compression — Apache Parquet's zstd at max level is optimal for all production workloads
Always default settings — Parquet defaults are tuned for data engineering and should never be changed
Tune by analogy to other Alliance tables — apply the most-used team's settings to new tables
questions.correctAnswer: Measure I/O with default → change one parameter → re-measure → compare. Never tune by intuition
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: 1MB → 256KB pages helps 5%-selective filters but not 50%-selective. Same mistake as M8 mid-level: optimization without measurement is intuition guessing.

questions.question: What does Delta Lake time travel enable for incident recovery?
options: Read version 84 (last correct), recompute, write version 86 — 5min recovery vs 2h backup restore
Time travel is purely a feature for data scientists — incident recovery uses traditional backups
Time travel deletes corrupt data automatically — Delta Lake detects and removes bad versions on its own
Time travel is encrypted by default — only authorized users can read historical versions of tables
questions.correctAnswer: Read version 84 (last correct), recompute, write version 86 — 5min recovery vs 2h backup restore
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: every write = new version in transaction log. French billing aggregation bug = read version 84, recompute, write 86. 30-day retention.

questions.question: What does an Iceberg catalog enable?
options: Multi-engine access (Python pipeline + Spark + Trino) on the same table without format conversion
Encrypted access — Iceberg catalogs are the only table format that supports column-level encryption
Vendor lock-in to Apache — only Apache-licensed engines can connect to an Iceberg catalog instance
Real-time replication — Iceberg catalogs replicate tables across regions for high availability
questions.correctAnswer: Multi-engine access (Python pipeline + Spark + Trino) on the same table without format conversion
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: REST catalog/Hive Metastore/AWS Glue/Nessie. Decouples table from engine. Python (delta-rs/pyiceberg), Trino, Spark all see consistent view.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance run both Delta Lake AND Iceberg?
options: Delta = Python (delta-rs, no JVM); Iceberg = Trino analytics (catalog-native). Best-of-breed per use case
Delta is for staging, Iceberg for production — environment-specific table formats per Apache standards
Delta is for billing, Iceberg for analytics — domain-specific formats prevent cross-domain coupling
Both formats are required by Lithuanian regulators for any cross-country data platform globally
questions.correctAnswer: Delta = Python (delta-rs, no JVM); Iceberg = Trino analytics (catalog-native). Best-of-breed per use case
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: dual-format cost = double op knowledge. Justified because each serves different teams with different tools. Single-format would sacrifice either delta-rs MERGE or Trino integration.

questions.question: How does the Alliance review storage tier boundaries?
options: Quarterly review checks hot-tier hit rate; > 5% fall-through extends hot window (year 1: 30 → 45 days)
Annual review only — boundaries are stable enough to need only one review per year per tier
Never reviewed — Apache standards set boundaries that should not be changed regardless of usage
Daily review — boundaries shift constantly based on the previous day's query patterns automatically
questions.correctAnswer: Quarterly review checks hot-tier hit rate; > 5% fall-through extends hot window (year 1: 30 → 45 days)
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: 30-day boundary assumed billing accesses last 30 days. French team added 45-day reconciliation = boundary extended. Quarterly review prevents 10× latency surprises.

questions.question: Why did the Alliance migrate from raw Parquet to Delta Lake?
options: Concurrent writers + readers safety (ACID) — not for table format features per se but multi-access pattern
Delta Lake is faster than raw Parquet — Apache benchmarks show 5× improvement on all queries
Delta Lake is required by Apache standards for any data platform exceeding 10 TB of storage
Delta Lake supports SQL — raw Parquet has no SQL interface for analytical queries at scale
questions.correctAnswer: Concurrent writers + readers safety (ACID) — not for table format features per se but multi-access pattern
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: raw Parquet readers may see partial files concurrent writers create. Delta Lake's transaction log = consistent snapshots. Multi-access pattern needs ACID.

questions.question: What is the table format decision criterion at the Alliance?
options: Access pattern complexity (concurrency, updates, multi-engine) — not data volume
Always use a table format — Apache standards require Delta Lake or Iceberg for all storage
Never use a table format — raw Parquet is simpler and sufficient for all data engineering use cases
Always use Delta Lake — Iceberg is too complex for production use at any data platform scale
questions.correctAnswer: Access pattern complexity (concurrency, updates, multi-engine) — not data volume
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: smallest Alliance Delta = 50MB lookup table updated by 3 teams. Largest raw Parquet = 200GB Bronze. Size and format are independent dimensions.

questions.question: How does Module 8 build on Module 7's storage architecture?
options: RBAC for Delta tables, column-level encryption for PII Parquet, GDPR for cross-country, audit logs
M8 replaces M7 entirely — security architecture supersedes storage architecture at scale
M8 is independent of M7 — security and storage are unrelated concerns at the senior level
M8 deprecates M7 — multi-tenant security eliminates the need for storage architecture decisions
questions.correctAnswer: RBAC for Delta tables, column-level encryption for PII Parquet, GDPR for cross-country, audit logs
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: storage tiering → audit log retention. Time travel → right-to-erasure verification. Storage and security are inseparable at enterprise scale.

questions.question: Why is the Alliance's security model woven into platform architecture, not bolted on?
options: Module 1's topology = jurisdictional compliance, M4's authz = classification, M5's registry = access impact
Security is a separate concern from architecture — security teams design it independently of platform teams
Lithuanian regulations require security to be designed first — architecture follows security decisions
Apache standards mandate post-hoc security — security must be added after the platform is operational
questions.correctAnswer: Module 1's topology = jurisdictional compliance, M4's authz = classification, M5's registry = access impact
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: previous modules implemented security implicitly. This module formalizes the complete architecture, compliance framework, and audit infrastructure that demonstrates compliance.

questions.question: What is the rule for wildcard access grants at the Alliance?
options: Restricted to team's own namespace (ie.billing.* OK, ie.* requires platform team approval)
Wildcards are forbidden — every grant must specify exact resource names without any wildcard
Wildcards are unrestricted — engineers can grant cross-team access freely without any approval
Wildcards require Apache certification — only Apache-certified engineers can create wildcard grants
questions.correctAnswer: Restricted to team's own namespace (ie.billing.* OK, ie.* requires platform team approval)
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: ie.* gives access to every Irish resource including other teams'. Cross-namespace wildcards = platform approval. CI flags these for review.

questions.question: What is the role of the Alliance's PII scanner vs the data product owner's classification?
options: Scanner is automated first line; owner's decision is authoritative determination — both needed
Scanner replaces the owner's decision — automation is more accurate than human judgment for PII
Owner's decision replaces the scanner — automated scanning produces too many false positives
Both are independent — scanner runs in CI, owner reviews quarterly, results never cross
questions.correctAnswer: Scanner is automated first line; owner's decision is authoritative determination — both needed
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: scanner catches obvious patterns. Cannot detect semantic PII (meter_id correlated with public address DB). Scanner reduces accidents; human decides PII in domain.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance's GDPR erasure pipeline cover backup snapshots and log files?
options: Customer data exists in Delta tables, raw Parquet, Redis cache, structlog JSON, backups, registry samples
GDPR requires erasure only from primary storage — backups and logs are excluded from the right to erasure
Apache requires erasure from databases only — file-based storage is exempt from GDPR Article 17
Erasure is purely conceptual — no actual data deletion is required by GDPR Article 17 in practice
questions.correctAnswer: Customer data exists in Delta tables, raw Parquet, Redis cache, structlog JSON, backups, registry samples
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: data catalog tracks every store with customer-identifiable data. Erasure processes each. Quarterly audit verifies no missing store = compliance gap detected.

questions.question: Why does the Alliance design for UK adequacy revocation as a non-event for analytics?
options: Cross-border analytics use only anonymized Gold data — only 3 specific PII flows need SCC fallback
UK adequacy is permanent — Apache standards guarantee adequacy across all EU-UK data transfers
Analytics teams are exempt from adequacy — only billing teams need adequacy considerations globally
Adequacy revocation triggers automatic platform shutdown — no design considerations are needed
questions.correctAnswer: Cross-border analytics use only anonymized Gold data — only 3 specific PII flows need SCC fallback
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: design for worst case at start = costs nothing. Redesign after revocation = months of emergency engineering. Anonymized aggregates make analytics adequacy-independent.

questions.question: What is meta-audit at the Alliance?
options: Access to the audit log is itself audit-logged — required by DPO for GDPR compliance because log is confidential
Apache certification of the audit logging system — only Apache-certified loggers can be used in production
Recursive testing of audit log replication — tests that the audit log replicates audit log access events
Random sampling of audit events — meta-audit checks 5% of events for accuracy and completeness
questions.correctAnswer: Access to the audit log is itself audit-logged — required by DPO for GDPR compliance because log is confidential
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: audit log records customer_id in erasure events + actor_identity. Classified confidential. audit:read scope (platform + compliance teams). Access logged recursively.

questions.question: What does the Alliance's quarterly security drill simulate?
options: Secret compromise: revoke test secret, measure time to full recovery, identify procedure gaps
Production outage: shut down all services for 1 hour to test the disaster recovery playbook fully
Penetration test: external red team attempts unauthorized access to find exploitable vulnerabilities
Compliance audit: regulators arrive unannounced to inspect all security controls and audit logs
questions.correctAnswer: Secret compromise: revoke test secret, measure time to full recovery, identify procedure gaps
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: untested revocation procedure fails under pressure. Drill: revoke test secret, measure recovery, fix gaps. Recorded in security review minutes.

questions.question: What is zero-trust at the Alliance?
options: Every access authenticated + authorized regardless of network location (combination of token + RBAC + network + audit)
Network firewall + VPN — once inside the network, all access is implicitly trusted at the Alliance
Single sign-on for all engineers — one login grants access to all systems without re-authentication
Apache zero-trust certification — Apache provides a turnkey zero-trust solution for all platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Every access authenticated + authorized regardless of network location (combination of token + RBAC + network + audit)
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: not a firewall. Design principle. Service tokens (auth) + RBAC (authz) + network policies (defense in depth) + audit (detection). Removing any breaks zero-trust.

questions.question: What does the annual classification review check at the Alliance?
options: Under-classified (PII in internal datasets via PII scanner) AND over-classified (public marked confidential)
Only under-classified data — over-classification is harmless because more security is always better
Only over-classified data — under-classification is automatically caught by the PII scanner daily
questions.correctAnswer: Under-classified (PII in internal datasets via PII scanner) AND over-classified (public marked confidential)
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: under-classified = PII exposure to unauthorized teams. Over-classified = wasted engineering time + audit capacity. Both are misclassifications.

questions.question: How does Module 9 build on Module 8's security architecture?
options: Kafka SASL/SCRAM (auth) + topic ACLs (namespace authz) + audit-logged messages — streaming equivalent of M8
M9 replaces M8 — streaming architecture supersedes batch security entirely at enterprise scale
M9 is independent of M8 — streaming has no security requirements that batch does not already cover
M9 deprecates M8 — exactly-once delivery patterns eliminate the need for security controls
questions.correctAnswer: Kafka SASL/SCRAM (auth) + topic ACLs (namespace authz) + audit-logged messages — streaming equivalent of M8
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: SASL/SCRAM = streaming JWT. Topic ACLs = namespace-scoped permissions. Every consumed message audit-logged. Security applies directly to streaming.

questions.question: How does the Alliance treat streaming relative to its 197+ batch pipelines?
options: Extension for specific use cases where batch latency is insufficient — not replacement
Replacement — Apache standards require migrating all batch to streaming for modern platforms
Forbidden — Apache standards prohibit streaming in any production data platform globally
Required for everything — every pipeline must be streaming regardless of latency requirements
questions.correctAnswer: Extension for specific use cases where batch latency is insufficient — not replacement
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: 197 batch pipelines unchanged. 3 streaming pipelines added for sub-minute latency. Module teaches adding streaming to batch platform, not migration.

questions.question: What is the rule about Kafka partition counts at the Alliance?
options: Can be added but never removed; adding breaks key-to-partition mapping for keyed messages
Auto-scales based on throughput — Kafka adjusts partition count automatically every hour
Always 1 partition per topic — single-partition topics are simpler for Python consumers to handle
Apache standards mandate exactly 16 partitions per topic for any production Kafka deployment
questions.correctAnswer: Can be added but never removed; adding breaks key-to-partition mapping for keyed messages
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: SCADA topic 3 partitions = 3 countries (unlikely to change). Meter events 8 partitions = sub-region count with growth room. Set once, stable for 18 months.

questions.question: What does the Alliance's Kafka consumer set `max.poll.interval.ms` to for batched Delta Lake writes?
options: 600 seconds (10 min) — accommodates batch writes of up to 100,000 messages per micro-batch
30 seconds — Apache standards for any Python Kafka consumer regardless of workload type
60 seconds — the protocol's default, increased only with explicit Apache certification approval
Infinite — disable timeout entirely so the consumer can process arbitrarily large batches safely
questions.correctAnswer: 600 seconds (10 min) — accommodates batch writes of up to 100,000 messages per micro-batch
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: default 300s (5min). Batch write of 100K messages exceeds default → rebalance kicks in → reprocessing. Set to 600s for 10-min Delta MERGE windows.

questions.question: What does end-to-end exactly-once mean at the Alliance?
options: Each turbine event appears exactly once in Delta Lake AND exactly once on operations dashboard
Kafka exactly-once between producer and consumer — Delta Lake write delivery is best-effort beyond Kafka
Each event processed exactly once on each Celery worker — duplicates across workers are acceptable
Apache Kafka guarantees exactly-once for all messages — no additional patterns are needed
questions.correctAnswer: Each turbine event appears exactly once in Delta Lake AND exactly once on operations dashboard
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: Kafka exactly-once is between Kafka boundary. Alliance exactly-once = end-to-end. Delta MERGE is idempotent (achieves it for 2/3 use cases). Billing uses transactional.

questions.question: What does Debezium capture from PostgreSQL?
options: WAL (write-ahead log) — converts INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE into structured Kafka events near-real-time
Trigger output — Debezium installs SQL triggers on every table to capture changes
Periodic snapshots — Debezium polls PostgreSQL every minute to detect changes
Apache standard CDC events — only certified Apache databases support Debezium properly
questions.correctAnswer: WAL (write-ahead log) — converts INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE into structured Kafka events near-real-time
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: WAL is PostgreSQL's own change log. Debezium reads it, publishes events. Python consumer MERGEs into Silver Delta within seconds of each change.

questions.question: How does the Alliance refresh the in-memory turbine metadata lookup without blocking?
options: Background thread loads new dict, atomic pointer swap (thread-safe in CPython due to GIL)
Synchronous refresh in the consumer poll loop — adds 200ms latency but is simpler to implement
Redis cache with 300s TTL — every event read triggers a Redis lookup for current metadata
Apache standards require periodic restart of consumers — refresh by killing and recreating workers
questions.correctAnswer: Background thread loads new dict, atomic pointer swap (thread-safe in CPython due to GIL)
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: synchronous = 200ms latency hit. Background thread + atomic swap = 0 latency. Pointer swap is thread-safe due to GIL (M2 lesson 5).

questions.question: How does the Alliance describe the hybrid batch + streaming architecture?
options: Steady-state architecture — batch optimal for high-volume/latency-tolerant; streaming for low-volume/latency-sensitive
Transitional — the Alliance is migrating to pure streaming over the next 2 years per Apache standards
Legacy — modern data platforms have abandoned batch entirely in favor of pure streaming everywhere
Required by regulators — Lithuanian law mandates dual batch + streaming for all data platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Steady-state architecture — batch optimal for high-volume/latency-tolerant; streaming for low-volume/latency-sensitive
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: pure batch fails grid ops (27h latency for turbine status). Pure streaming fails billing (unnecessary complexity). Hybrid matches workload to optimal model.

questions.question: What is the Alliance's early-warning indicator for streaming consumer capacity?
options: Lag growth rate (msg/s produced minus consumed) — positive sustained = needs more capacity before data loss
Absolute lag — Apache standards trigger alerts only when lag exceeds 100,000 messages on any partition
Consumer crash count — capacity issues manifest as crashes, not as lag accumulation gradually
CPU utilization — streaming consumers are CPU-bound so CPU is the only relevant capacity metric
questions.correctAnswer: Lag growth rate (msg/s produced minus consumed) — positive sustained = needs more capacity before data loss
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: 200/s produced - 180/s consumed = 20/s growth = 1.7M/day. 7-day retention = data loss after ~12M messages. Growth rate Prometheus metric, 1h evaluation.

questions.question: What is the Alliance's criterion for streaming over batch?
options: Traceable to a business decision that cannot be made with stale data (grid curtailment, real-time billing)
Always streaming — sub-minute latency is the goal for every data product at the Alliance
Streaming for new pipelines, batch for legacy — gradual migration over many quarters and years
Streaming for popular pipelines, batch for niche ones — popularity drives latency requirements
questions.correctAnswer: Traceable to a business decision that cannot be made with stale data (grid curtailment, real-time billing)
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: grid ops needs sub-10s (delayed curtailment = grid instability). Billing needs sub-5min (stale prices = €3K/h cost). Analytics dropped streaming = no decision needs sub-minute.

questions.question: How does Module 10 close the senior track?
options: Maps 30 modules across 3 tracks into unified governance: catalog, costs, SLA, capacity, organizational patterns
Module 10 is independent of all prior modules — it covers organizational topics unrelated to engineering
Module 10 replaces all prior modules — governance is the only thing that matters at enterprise scale
Module 10 deprecates the senior track — modern platforms use only modules 1-9 in production
questions.correctAnswer: Maps 30 modules across 3 tracks into unified governance: catalog, costs, SLA, capacity, organizational patterns
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: complete journey junior (400K, 1 team) → mid (1.2M, mid scale) → senior (5M, multi-country). M10 maps all 30 contributions into governance + decommissioning.

questions.question: What does Lesson 1 say makes 200 pipelines a 'platform' rather than 200 independent scripts?
options: Shared conventions (M1), schemas (M5), security (M8), accountability (this module) = platform
Storage location — pipelines on the same disk are a platform; pipelines on different disks are not
Programming language — Python pipelines are a platform; mixed-language pipelines are independent scripts
Number of teams — pipelines run by one team are a platform; pipelines run by multiple teams are scripts
questions.correctAnswer: Shared conventions (M1), schemas (M5), security (M8), accountability (this module) = platform
questions.explanation: Lesson 1: 200 scripts share infra. Platform shares conventions, schemas, security, accountability. Governance makes whole > sum of parts.

questions.question: How does the Alliance measure data catalog usability?
options: Search success rate (80% target) and time-to-discovery (< 2 min target) — tracked via API access logs
Number of entries — more entries = better catalog (Apache standards measure size, not usability)
Engineer survey — usability is purely subjective so qualitative feedback is the only metric
Entry update frequency — daily updates = active catalog; less frequent = stale and unused catalog
questions.correctAnswer: Search success rate (80% target) and time-to-discovery (< 2 min target) — tracked via API access logs
questions.explanation: Lesson 2: stale + missing + broken = engineers distrust, fall back to Slack. 80%/2-min target. Below = catalog team investigates which entries are bad.

questions.question: What does the Alliance enforce in CI for data lineage?
options: Every read call references a dataset in catalog's upstream_dependencies; missing reference fails build
Every read is logged for 30 days — lineage is reconstructed from access logs at quarterly review
Every read is encrypted — lineage gaps are detected by analyzing the encrypted access patterns
Lineage is not enforced — Apache standards leave lineage entirely to the data engineer's discretion
questions.correctAnswer: Every read call references a dataset in catalog's upstream_dependencies; missing reference fails build
questions.explanation: Lesson 3: undeclared read = missing edge in lineage graph = schema change breaks pipeline undetected. CI: 'Pipeline reads from S but doesn't declare dependency'.

questions.question: Why is cost allocation informational (not budget-linked) in Year 1 at the Alliance?
options: Awareness + optimization incentive without adversarial dynamics — Year 2 plans budget linkage
Apache PEP 8 forbids budget-linked chargeback in any production data platform globally enforced
Lithuanian regulators require Year 1 information-only chargeback for all multi-team data platforms
Budget linkage is technically infeasible — Year 1 builds the data; Year 2 will use it for budgets
questions.correctAnswer: Awareness + optimization incentive without adversarial dynamics — Year 2 plans budget linkage
questions.explanation: Lesson 4: penalizing legitimate usage (billing 40% of compute because 40% of data) discourages data-intensive work. Year 1 awareness, Year 2 budget enforcement.

questions.question: How does end-to-end SLA availability compound across pipeline dependencies?
options: Multiplicatively — Bronze 99.5% × Silver 99% × Gold 99% × API 99.9% = 97.4% end-to-end
Additively — sum of dependency availabilities provides the end-to-end value at the platform layer
Maximum — end-to-end equals the highest availability among all the pipeline's dependencies always
Minimum — end-to-end equals the lowest availability among all the pipeline's dependencies always
questions.correctAnswer: Multiplicatively — Bronze 99.5% × Silver 99% × Gold 99% × API 99.9% = 97.4% end-to-end
questions.explanation: Lesson 5: weakest link determines chain. Compounded multiplication = realistic. Bronze 99.5% (2 fails/month) is the weakest; bringing Bronze higher raises everything.

questions.question: What does the Alliance learn from Year 1's capacity planning estimates?
options: First year ±20% error from estimates; second year ±8% from actual metrics — €600/month over-provisioning
Estimates are perfect — Apache standards capacity planning has zero error in the first year always
Estimates are useless — Apache standards recommend skipping Year 1 capacity planning entirely
Estimates are confidential — Lithuanian regulators forbid sharing capacity data across teams or years
questions.correctAnswer: First year ±20% error from estimates; second year ±8% from actual metrics — €600/month over-provisioning
questions.explanation: Lesson 6: 15% meter growth est, 18% actual. 20 new pipelines/year est, 24 actual. Year 2 metric-based = ±5% provisioning. Over-provisioning = cost of estimation uncertainty.

questions.question: How does the Alliance handle deprecated and sunset datasets in the catalog?
options: Filtered by default — visible only with explicit toggle. Keeps engineer's view focused on 400 active
Auto-deleted after 7 days of zero consumer activity per Apache standards across all platforms
Highlighted prominently — deprecated datasets are shown first in search results to draw attention
Marked with red border — visual styling alone is sufficient to indicate dataset lifecycle status
questions.correctAnswer: Filtered by default — visible only with explicit toggle. Keeps engineer's view focused on 400 active
questions.explanation: Lesson 7: 100 legacy + 400 active = clutter, confusion, audit overhead. Filter by default = focused view. Toggle preserves access for historical analysis and compliance.

questions.question: What distinguishes an enabling team from a support team at the Alliance?
options: Enabling: proactively closes capability gaps. Support: reacts to tickets. Enabling = SDK adoption, not tickets
Enabling teams have more engineers — staffing alone determines whether a team is enabling or support
Enabling teams have more Apache certifications — certification levels dictate the team's classification
Enabling and support are equivalent — Apache standards do not distinguish between the two patterns
questions.correctAnswer: Enabling: proactively closes capability gaps. Support: reacts to tickets. Enabling = SDK adoption, not tickets
questions.explanation: Lesson 8: support = tickets. Enabling = notices 4 teams' custom retry logic, creates tutorial, migrates them to SDK. Success = capabilities adopted, not tickets resolved.

questions.question: What is the 85/10/5 split at the Alliance?
options: 85% changes need no governance, 10% standard process, 5% full governance — engineers spend < 5% on governance
85% pipelines, 10% APIs, 5% notebooks — distribution of artifact types across the platform overall
85% Python, 10% SQL, 5% other — language distribution across the entire codebase at the Alliance
85% Apache-approved, 10% commercial, 5% in-house — software origin distribution per platform standards
questions.correctAnswer: 85% changes need no governance, 10% standard process, 5% full governance — engineers spend < 5% on governance
questions.explanation: Lesson 9: every change needing approval = bottleneck (M1 lesson 4 anti-pattern). No change needing approval = unprotected contracts. 85/10/5 = scoped governance.

questions.question: What does Lesson 10 say is the complete journey from junior to senior?
options: From 'I can write code that processes data' to 'I can architect data infrastructure organizations depend on'
From writing pipelines to writing pipelines but in Rust — language migration is the senior shift
From Python to Scala — Apache standards require senior engineers to use the JVM ecosystem fully
From individual contributor to manager — senior data engineering is purely a management role globally
questions.correctAnswer: From 'I can write code that processes data' to 'I can architect data infrastructure organizations depend on'
questions.explanation: Lesson 10: junior built pipelines. Mid operated them. Senior designs platform. Platform = conventions + tools + governance + organizational patterns that make pipelines possible.
